greek_head: Κατὰ τὴν χθὲς ὁμολογίαν ὦ Σώκρατες ἥκομεν αὐτοί τε κοσμίως καὶ τόνδε τινὰ ξένον ἄγομεν τὸ μὲν
greek_tokens: kata o xqes omologia eimi swkraths hkw autos te kosmios kai ode tis cenos agw o men genos ek elea etairos de o amfi parmenidhs kai zhnwn etairos mala de anhr filosofos ara oun eimi qeodwros ou cenos alla tis qeos agw kata o omhros logos lanqanw os fhmi allos
english_head: According to our yesterday’s agreement, Socrates, we have come ourselves, as we were bound to do, and we bring also this man with us; he is a stranger from Elea, one of the followers of Parmenides and Zeno, and a real philosopher. Are you not unwittingly bringing, as Homer says, some god, and no mere stranger, Theodorus? He says

greek_head: τε θεοὺς τοῖς ἀνθρώποις ὁπόσοι μετέχουσιν αἰδοῦς δικαίας καὶ δὴ καὶ τὸν ξένιον οὐχ ἥκιστα θεὸν συνοπαδὸν γιγνόμενον
greek_tokens: te qeos o anqrwpos oposos metexw aidw dikaios kai dh kai o cenios ou hkistos qeos sunopados gignomai ubris te kai eunomia o anqrwpos kaqoraw taxa oun an kai sos tis outos o kreisswn sunepomai faulos egw eimi en o logos eporaw te kai elegxw qeos eimi tis elegktikos ou outos o tropos eimi swkraths o cenos alla metrios o peri o eris spoudazw kai egw dokew qeos men anhr oudamos eimi qeios meis pas
english_head: that the gods, and especially the god of strangers, enter into companionship with men who have a share of due reverence and that they behold the deeds, both violent and righteous, of mankind. So perhaps this companion of yours may be one of the higher powers, who comes to watch over and refute us because we are worthless in argument—a kind of god of refutation. No, Socrates, that is not the stranger’s character; he is more reasonable than those who devote themselves to disputation. And though I

greek_head: γὰρ ἐγὼ τοὺς φιλοσόφους τοιούτους προσαγορεύω Καὶ καλῶς γε ὦ φίλε τοῦτο μέντοι κινδυνεύει τὸ
greek_tokens: gar egw o filosofos toioutos prosagoreuw kai kalos ge eimi filos outos mentoi kinduneuw o genos ou polus tis raios ws epos eipon eimi diakrinw h o o qeaomai panu gar anhr outos pantoios fantazomai dia o o allos agnoia epistrwfaw polis e mh plastos alla ontws filosofos kaqoraw uyoqen o o katos bios kai o men dokew eimi o mhdeis timios o de acios o pas kai tote men politikos
english_head: I certainly do think he is divine, for I give that epithet to all philosophers. And rightly, my friend. However, I fancy it is not much easier, if I may say so, to recognize this class, than that of the gods. For these men—I mean those who are not feignedly but really philosophers—appear disguised in all sorts of shapes, thanks to the ignorance of the rest of mankind, and visit the cities,Hom. Od. 17.485-7 beholding from above the life of those below, and they seem to some to be of no worth and

greek_head: φαντάζονται τοτὲ δὲ σοφισταί τοτὲ δ' ἔστιν οἷς δόξαν παράσχοιντ' ἂν ὡς παντάπασιν ἔχοντες μανικῶς τοῦ μέντοι
greek_tokens: fantazomai tote de sofisths tote de eimi os doca parexw an ws pantapasi exw manikos o mentoi cenos egw hdews an punqanomai ei filos autos tis outos
english_head: and sometimes as sophists, and sometimes they may give some people the impression that they are altogether mad. But I should like to ask our stranger here, if agreeable to him, what people in his country thought about these matters,

greek_head: οἱ περὶ τὸν ἐκεῖ τόπον ἡγοῦντο καὶ ὠνόμαζον Τὰ ποῖα δή
greek_tokens: e peri o ekei topos ageomai kai onomazw o poios dh sofisths politikos filosofos tis de malista kai o poios tis peri autos diaporew eromai dianoeomai ode poteros eis pas outos nomizw h duo h kaqa o onoma treis treis kai o genos diairew kaqa eis onoma genos ekastos prosaptw alla oudeis ws oimh fqonos autos dierxomai autos h pws eimi cenos legw
english_head: and what names they used. What matters do you mean? Sophist, statesman, philosopher. What particular difficulty and what kind of difficulty in regard to them is it about which you had in mind to ask? It is this: Did they consider all these one, or two, or, as there are three names, did they divide them into three classes and ascribe to each a class, corresponding to a single name? I think he has no objection to talking about them. What do you say, stranger?

greek_head: Οὕτως ὦ Θεόδωρε φθόνος μὲν γὰρ οὐδεὶς οὐδὲ χαλεπὸν εἰπεῖν ὅτι γε τρί' ἡγοῦντο καθ' ἕκαστον μὴν διορίσασθαι
greek_tokens: outws eimi qeodwros fqonos men gar oudeis oude xalepos eipon ostis ge treis ageomai kaqa ekastos meis diorizw safhs tis pote eimi ou mikros oude radios ergnumi kai men dh kata tuxh ge eimi swkraths logos epilambanw paraplew os kai prin egw deuro erxomai dierwtaw autos tugxanw o de tautos o pros sos nun kai tote skhptw pros egw epei diakouw ge fhmi ikanos kai ou amnhmonew
english_head: Just what you did, Theodorus; for I have no objection, and it is not difficult to say that they considered them three. But it is no small or easy task to define clearly the nature of each. The fact is, Socrates, that by chance you have hit upon a question very like what we happened to be asking him before we came here; and he made excuses to us then, as he does now to you; though he admits that he has heard it thoroughly discussed and remembers what he heard.

greek_head: Μὴ τοίνυν ὦ ξένε ἡμῶν τήν γε πρώτην αἰτησάντων χάριν ἀπαρνηθεὶς γένῃ τοσόνδε δ' ἡμῖν φράζε πότερον
greek_tokens: mh toinun eimi cenos egw o ge proteros aitew xaris aparneomai gignomai tososde de egw frazw poteros eqw hdus autos epi sautou makros logos dieceimi legos outos o an endeiknumi tis boulomai h dia erwthsis oios pote kai parmenidhs xraomai kai dieceimi logos pagkalos paragignomai egw neos eimi ekeinos mala dh tote eimi presbuths
english_head: In that case, stranger, do not refuse us the first favor we have asked; but just tell us this: Do you generally prefer to expound in a long uninterrupted speech of your own whatever you wish to explain to anyone, or do you prefer the method of questions? I was present once when Parmenides employed the latter method and carried on a splendid discussion. I was a young man then, and he was very old. The method of dialogue, Socrates, is easier

greek_head: Τῷ μέν ὦ Σώκρατες ἀλύπως τε καὶ εὐηνίως προςδιαλεγομένῳ ῥᾷον οὕτω τὸ πρὸς ἄλλον εἰ δὲ μή τὸ καθ'
greek_tokens: o men eimi swkraths alupos te kai euhnios prosdialegomai raios outws o pros allos ei de mh o kaqa eautou ecesti toinun o pareimi os an boulomai eklegw pas gar upakouw su praos sumboulos meis egw xraomai o neos tis airew qeaithtos ode h kai o allos ei tis su kata new eimi swkraths aidws tis egw exis o nun proteros suggignomai su mh kata mikros epos pros epos poiew
english_head: with an interlocutor who is tractable and gives no trouble; but otherwise I prefer the continuous speech by one person. Well, you may choose whomever you please of those present; they will all respond pleasantly to you; but if you take my advice you will choose one of the young fellows, Theaetetus here, or any of the others who suits you. Socrates, this is the first time I have come among you, and I am somewhat ashamed, instead of carrying on the discussion by merely giving brief replies to your

greek_head: τὴν συνουσίαν ἀλλ' ἐκτείναντα ἀπομηκύνειν λόγον συχνὸν κατ' ἐμαυτόν εἴτε καὶ πρὸς ἕτερον οἷον ἐπίδειξιν ποιούμενον
greek_tokens: o sunousia alla ekteinw apomhkunw logos suxnos kata emautou eite kai pros eteros oios epideicis poiew o gar eimi o nun erw ou osos wde erwtaw elpizw an autos eimi tis alla tugxanw logos pammhkhs eimi o de au sos mh xarizomai kai ode allos te kai sew legw ws eipon acenos tis katafainw egw kai
english_head: or in reply to another, as if I were giving an exhibition; but I must, for really the present subject is not what one might expect from the form of the question, but is a matter for very long speech. On the other hand it seems unfriendly and discourteous to refuse a favor to you and these gentlemen, especially when you have spoken as you did. As for

greek_head: ἄγριον ἐπεὶ Θεαίτητόν γε τὸν προσδιαλεγόμενον εἶναι δέχομαι παντάπασιν ἐξ ὧν αὐτός τε πρότερον διείλεγμαι καὶ
greek_tokens: agrios epei qeaithtos ge o prosdialegomai eimi dexomai pantapasi ek os autos te proteros dialegw kai su o nun egw diakeleuomai draw toinun eimi cenos outws kai kaqa eipon swkratew pas xarizomai eimi kinduneuw pros men outos oudeis eti lekteos eimi qeaithtos pros de sos hdh o meta outos ws eoika gignomai an o logos an de ara tis o mhkos ponew axqomai mh egw aitiaomai outos alla ode o sos etairos
english_head: Theaetetus I accept him most willingly as interlocutor in view of my previous conversation with him and of your present recommendation. But, stranger, by taking this course and following Socrates’s suggestion will you please the others too? I am afraid there is nothing more to be said about that, Theaetetus; but from now on, my talk will, I fancy, be addressed to you. And if you get tired and are bored by the length of the talk, do not blame me, but these friends of yours.

greek_head: Ἀλλ' οἶμαι μὲν δὴ νῦν οὕτως οὐκ ἀπερεῖν ἂν δ' ἄρα τι τοιοῦτον γίγνηται καὶ τόνδε παραληψόμεθα Σωκράτη
greek_tokens: alla oimh men dh nun outws ou aperaw an de ara tis toioutos gignomai kai ode paralambanw swkratew o swkraths men omwnumos emos de hlikiwths kai suggumnasths os sundiaponew meta egw o polus ou ahqhs eu legw kai outos men idios bouleusis proeimi o logos koinh de meta egw su suskepteon arxw proteros ws egw fainw nun apo o sofisths zhtew
english_head: Oh, no, I do not think I shall get tired of it so easily, but if such a thing does happen, we will call in this Socrates, the namesake of the other Socrates; he is of my own age and my companion in the gymnasium, and is in the habit of working with me in almost everything. Very well; you will follow your own devices about that as the discussion proceeds; but now you and I must investigate in common, beginning first, as it seems to me, with the sophist, and must search out and make plain

greek_head: καὶ ἐμφανίζοντι λόγῳ τί ποτ' ἔστι νῦν γὰρ δὴ σύ τε κἀγὼ τούτου πέρι τοὔνομα μόνον ἔχομεν κοινῇ τὸ δὲ ἔργον ἐφ'
greek_tokens: kai emfanizw logos tis pote eimi nun gar dh su te agos outos peri onoma monos exw koinh o de ergnumi epi os kalew ekateros taxa an idios para egw autos exw deomai de aei pas peri o pragma autos mallon dia logos h onoma monos sunomologew xwris logos o de fulon o nun epinoew zhtew ou pas radios sullambanw tis pote eimi o sofisths osos de au o megas deomai diaponew kalos peri o toioutos dokew pas kai pala
english_head: by argument what he is. For as yet you and I have nothing in common about him but the name; but as to the thing to which we give the name, we may perhaps each have a conception of it in our own minds; however, we ought always in every instance to come to agreement about the thing itself by argument rather than about the mere name without argument. But the tribe which we now intend to search for, the sophist, is not the easiest thing in the world to catch and define, and everyone has agreed long

greek_head: τὸ πρότερον ἐν σμικροῖς καὶ ῥᾴοσιν αὐτὰ δεῖν μελετᾶν πρὶν ἐν αὐτοῖς τοῖς μεγίστοις νῦν οὖν ὦ Θεαίτητε ἔγωγε καὶ
greek_tokens: o proteros en mikros kai radios autos dew meleth prin en autos o megas nun oun eimi qeaithtos egw kai egw outws sumbouleuw xalepos kai dusqhreutos hgeomai eimi o o sofisths genos proteros en allos radios o meqodos autos promeletaw ei mh su poqen eupeths exis eipon allos odos alla ou exw boulomai dhta peri tis o faulos meteimi peiraw paradeigma autos tiqhmi o megas
english_head: and easier matters before attacking the very greatest. So now, Theaetetus, this is my advice to ourselves, since we think the family of sophists is troublesome and hard to catch, that we first practise the method of hunting in something easier, unless you perhaps have some simpler way to suggest. I have not. Then shall we take some lesser thing and try to use it as a pattern for the greater?

greek_head: Ναί Τί δῆτα προταξαίμεθ' ἂν εὔγνωστον μὲν καὶ σμικρόν
greek_tokens: nai tis dhta prosagw an eugnwstos men kai mikros logos de mhdeis elasswn exw o megas oios aspalieuths ara ou pas te gnwrimos kai spoudazw ou panu tis polus tis epacios outws
english_head: Yes. Well, then, what example can we set before us which is well known and small, but no less capable of definition than any of the greater things? Say an angler; is he not known to all and unworthy of any great interest? Yes.

greek_head: Μέθοδον μὴν αὐτὸν ἐλπίζω καὶ λόγον οὐκ ἀνεπιτήδειον ἡμῖν ἔχειν πρὸς ὃ βουλόμεθα
greek_tokens: meqodos meis autos elpizw kai logos ou anepithdeios egw exw pros o boulomai kalos an exw ferw dh ode arxw autos kai egw legos poteros ws texniths autos h tis atexnos allos de dunamis exw tiqhmi hkistos ge atexnos alla meis o ge texnh pas sxedon eidos duo pws gewrgia men kai osos peri o qnhtos pas swma qerapeia o te au peri o sunqetos kai plastos o dh
english_head: But I hope he offers us a method and is capable of a definition not unsuitable to our purpose. That would be good. Come now; let us begin with him in this way: Tell me, shall we say that he is a man with an art, or one without an art, but having some other power? Certainly not one without an art. But of all arts there are, speaking generally, two kinds? How so? Agriculture and all kinds of care of any living beings, and that which has to do with things which are put together or molded

greek_head: σκεῦος ὠνομάκαμεν ἥ τε μιμητική σύμπαντα ταῦτα δικαιότατ' ἂν ἑνὶ προσαγορεύοιτ' ἂν ὀνόματι
greek_tokens: skeuos onomazw o te mimhtikos sumpas outos dikaios an eis prosagoreuw an onoma pws kai tis pas osper an mh proteros tis eimi usteron eis ousia agh o men agw poiew o de agw poiew pou fhmi orqos o de ge nundi o dierxomai apas exw eis outos o eautou dunamis exw gar oun poihtikos toinun autos sugkefalaiow proseipon
english_head: (utensils we call them), and the art of imitation—all these might properly be called by one name. How so, and what is the name? When anyone brings into being something which did not previously exist, we say that he who brings it into being produces it and that which is brought into being is produced. Certainly. Now all the arts which we have just mentioned direct their energy to production. Yes, they do. Let us, then, call these collectively the productive art.

greek_head: Ἔστω Τὸ δὴ μαθηματικὸν αὖ μετὰ τοῦτο εἶδος ὅλον καὶ
greek_tokens: eimi o dh maqhmatikos au meta outos eidos oloc kai o o gnwrisis o te xrhmatistikos kai agwnistikos kai qhreutikos epei dhmiourgew men oudeis outos o de eimi kai gignomai o men xeirow logos kai pracis o de o xeirow ou epitrepw malista an pou dia outos sunapas o mera texnh tis kthtikos legw an diaprepw nai prepw gar an
english_head: Agreed. And after this comes the whole class of learning and that of acquiring knowledge, and money making, and fighting, and hunting. None of these is creative, but they are all engaged in coercing, by deeds or words, things which already exist and have been produced, or in preventing others from coercing them; therefore all these divisions together might very properly be called acquisitive art. Yes, that would be proper. Then since acquisitive and productive art comprise

greek_head: Κτητικῆς δὴ καὶ ποιητικῆς συμπασῶν οὐσῶν τῶν τεχνῶν ἐν ποτέρᾳ τὴν ἀσπαλιευτικήν ὦ Θεαίτητε τιθῶμεν
greek_tokens: kthtikos dh kai poihtikos sumpas eimi o texnh en poteros o aspalieutikos eimi qeaithtos tiqhmi en kthtikos pou dhlos kthtikos de ara ou duo eidos o men ekwn pros ekwn metablhtikos eimi dia te dwrea kai misqwsis kai agorasis o de loipos h kata ergon h kata logos xeirow sumpas xeirwtikos an eaw fainw goun ek o erw tis de o xeirwtikos ara ou dixaw tmhteon ph
english_head: all the arts, in which, Theaetetus, shall we place the art of angling? In acquisitive art, clearly. And are there not two classes of acquisitive art—one the class of exchange between voluntary agents by means of gifts and wages and purchases, and the other, which comprises all the rest of acquisitive art, and, since it coerces either by word or deed, might be called coercive? It appears so, at any rate, from what you have said. Well then, shall we not divide coercive art into two parts? In what

greek_head: Τὸ μὲν ἀναφανδὸν ὅλον ἀγωνιστικὸν θέντας τὸ δὲ κρυφαῖον αὐτῆς πᾶν θηρευτικόν
greek_tokens: o men anafanda oloc agwnistikos tiqhmi o de krufaios autos pas qhreutikos nai o de ge meis qhreutikos alogos o mh ou temnw dixaw legos oph o men ayuxos genos diairew o de emyuxos tis meis eiper eimi ge amfw
english_head: and all the secret part hunting. Yes. But it would be unreasonable not to divide hunting into two parts. Say how it can be done. By dividing it into the hunting of the lifeless and of the living. Certainly, if both exist.

greek_head: Πῶς δὲ οὐκ ἔστον καὶ δεῖ γε ἡμᾶς τὸ μὲν τῶν ἀψύχων ἀνώνυμον ὂν πλὴν κατ' ἔνια τῆς κολυμβητικῆς
greek_tokens: pws de ou eimi kai deomai ge egw o men o ayuxos anwnumos eimi plhn kata enioi o kolumbhtikos atta mera kai toioutos allos braxus xairw eaw o de o emyuxos zwion eimi qhra proseipon zwoqhrikos eimi zwoqhrikos de ara ou diploos eidos an legw en dikh o men pezos genos polus eidos kai onoma diairew pezoqhrikos o de eteros neustikos zwion pas enugroqhrikos panu ge
english_head: Of course they exist. And we must pass over the hunting of lifeless things, which has no name, with the exception of some kinds of diving and the like, which are of little importance; but the hunting of living things we will call animal-hunting. Very well. And two classes of animal-hunting might properly be made, one (and this is divided under many classes and names) the hunting of creatures that go on their feet, land-animal hunting, and the other that of swimming creatures, to be called, as a

greek_head: Νευστικοῦ μὴν τὸ μὲν πτηνὸν φῦλον ὁρῶμεν τὸ δὲ ἔνυδρον
greek_tokens: neustikos meis o men pthnos fulon oraw o de enudros pws de ou kai o pthnos meis genos pas egw o qhra legw pou tis orniqeutikos legw gar oun o de enudros sxedon o sunolos alieutikos nai tis de outos au o qhra ara ou an kata megas mera duo diairew kata poios kaqa o o men erkos autoqen poiew o qhra o de plhssw pws legw kai ph diairew ekateros
english_head: And of swimming creatures we see that one tribe is winged and the other is in the water? Of course. And the hunting of winged creatures is called, as a whole, fowling. It is. And the hunting of water creatures goes by the general name of fishing. Yes. And might I not divide this kind of hunting into two principal divisions? What divisions? The one carries on the hunt by means of enclosures merely, the other by a blow. What do you mean, and how do you distinguish the two? As regards the first, be

greek_head: Τὸ μέν ὅτι πᾶν ὅσον ἂν ἕνεκα κωλύσεως εἴργῃ τι περιέχον ἕρκος εἰκὸς ὀνομάζειν
greek_tokens: o men ostis pas osos an eneka kwlusis ergw tis periexw erkos eoika onomazw panu men oun kurtos dh kai diktuon kai broxos kai porkos kai o toioutos mhs allos tis plhn erkos xrh prosagoreuw oudeis outos men ara erkoqhrikos o agra o meros fhmi h tis toioutos nai o de agkistron kai triodous plhssw gignomai
english_head: so as to constrain it is properly called an enclosure. Certainly. May not, then, wicker baskets and seines and snares and nets and the like be called enclosures? Assuredly. Then we will call this division hunting by enclosures, or something of that sort. Yes. And the other, which is done with a blow, by means of hooks and three pronged spears, we must now—to name it with a single word—

greek_head: ἕτερον μὲν ἐκείνου πληκτικὴν δέ τινα θήραν ἡμᾶς προςειπεῖν ἑνὶ λόγῳ νῦν χρεών ἢ τί τις ἄν ὦ Θεαίτητε εἴποι
greek_tokens: eteros men ekeinos plhktikos de tis qhra egw proseipon eis logos nun xrewn h tis tis an eimi qeaithtos eipon kallion amelew o onoma arkew gar kai outos o toinun plhktikos o men nukterinos oimh pros pur faos gignomai upo autos o peri o qhra pureutikos erw sumbainw panu ge o de ge meqhmerinos ws exw en akron agkistron kai o triodous pas agkistreutikos
english_head: call striking; or could a better name be found, Theaetetus? Never mind the name; that will do well enough. Then the kind of striking which takes place at night by the light of a fire is, I suppose, called by the hunters themselves fire-hunting. To be sure. And that which belongs to the daytime is, as a whole, barb-hunting, since the spears, as well as the hooks, are tipped with barbs.

greek_head: Λέγεται γὰρ οὖν Τοῦ τοίνυν ἀγκιστρευτικοῦ τῆς πληκτικῆς τὸ μὲν
greek_tokens: legw gar oun o toinun agkistreutikos o plhktikos o men anwqen eis o katos gignomai dia o o triodous outws malista xraomai triodontia tis oimh kalew fhmi goun tis o de ge loipos eimi eis eti monos ws eipon eidos o poios o o enantios outos plhgh agkistron te gignomai kai o ixqus ou h tis an tuxh o swma wsper
english_head: Yes, it is so called. Then of striking which belongs to barb-hunting, that part which proceeds downward from above, is called, because tridents are chiefly used in it, tridentry, I suppose. Yes, some people, at any rate, call it so. Then there still remains, I may say, only one further kind. What is that? The kind that is characterized by the opposite sort of blow, which is practised with a hook and strikes, not any chance part of the body of the fishes,

greek_head: τοῖς τριόδουσιν ἀλλὰ περὶ τὴν κεφαλὴν καὶ τὸ στόμα τοῦ θηρευθέντος ἑκάστοτε καὶ κάτωθεν εἰς τοὐναντίον ἄνω
greek_tokens: o triodous alla peri o kefalh kai o stoma o qhreuw ekastote kai katwqen eis enantion anoos rabdos kai kalamos anaspaw e tis fhmi eimi qeaithtos dew onoma legw dokazw men osper arti protiqhmi dew eceuriskw outos autos nun apotelew nun ara o aspalieutikos peri su te agos
english_head: as tridents do, but only the head and mouth of the fish caught, and proceeds from below upwards, being pulled up by twigs and rods. By what name, Theaetetus, shall we say this ought to be called? I think our search is now ended and we have found the very thing we set before us a while ago as necessary to find. Now, then, you and I are not only agreed

greek_head: συνωμολογήκαμεν οὐ μόνον τοὔνομα ἀλλὰ καὶ τὸν λόγον περὶ αὐτὸ τοὖργον εἰλήφαμεν ἱκανῶς συμπάσης γὰρ τέχνης
greek_tokens: sunomologew ou monos onoma alla kai o logos peri autos ergnumi lambanw ikanos sumpas gar texnh o men hmisus meros kthtikos eimi kthtikos de xeirwtikos xeirwtikos de qhreutikos o de qhreutikos zwoqhrikos zwoqhrikos de enugroqhrikos enugroqhrikos de o katwqen tmhma oloc alieutikos alieutikos de plhktikos plhktikos de agkistreutikos outos de o peri o katwqen
english_head: about the name of angling, but we have acquired also a satisfactory definition of the thing itself. For of art as a whole, half was acquisitive, and of the acquisitive, half was coercive, and of the coercive, half was hunting, and of hunting, half was animal hunting, and of animal hunting, half was water hunting, and, taken as a whole, of water hunting the lower part was fishing, and of fishing, half was striking, and of striking, half was barb-hunting, and of this the part in which the blow is

greek_head: ἄνω πληγὴν ἀνασπωμένην ἀπ' αὐτῆς τῆς πράξεως ἀφομοιωθὲν τοὔνομα ἡ νῦν ἀσπαλιευτικὴ ζητηθεῖσα ἐπίκλην
greek_tokens: anoos plhgh anaspaw apo autos o pracis afomoiow onoma o nun aspalieutikos zhtew epiklhn gignomai pantapasi men oun outos ge ikanos dhlow ferw dh kata outos o paradeigma kai o sofisths epixeirew euriskw ostis pote eimi komidh men oun kai meis ekeinos ge eimi o zhthma proteros poteros idiwths h tis texnh exw qeteos eimi o aspalieuths nai
english_head: has a name in the very likeness of the act and is called angling, which was the object of our present search. That at all events has been made perfectly clear. Come, then, let us use this as a pattern and try to find out what a sophist is. By all means. Well, then, the first question we asked was whether we must assume that the angler was just a man or was a man with an art. Yes. Now take this man of ours, Theaetetus.

greek_head: Καὶ νῦν δὴ τοῦτον ἰδιώτην θήσομεν ὦ Θεαίτητε ἢ παντάπασιν ὡς ἀληθῶς σοφιστήν
greek_tokens: kai nun dh outos idiwths tiqhmi eimi qeaithtos h pantapasi ws alhqhs sofisths oudamos idiwths manqanw gar o legw ws pas deomai toioutos eimi o ge onoma outos exw alla tis texnh autos egw exw ws eoika qeteos tis pote oun dh outos ara eimi pros qea agnoew anhr o anhr eimi suggeneus tis o o aspalieuths o sofisths ph qhreuths tis katafainw amfw egw
english_head: Shall we assume that he is just a man, or by all means really a man of wisdom? Certainly not just a man; for I catch your meaning that he is very far from being wise, although his name implies wisdom. But we must, it seems, assume that he has an art of some kind. Well, then, what in the world is this art that he has? Good gracious! Have we failed to notice that the man is akin to the other man? Who is akin to whom? The angler to the sophist. How so? They both seem clearly to me to be a sort of h

greek_head: Τίνος θήρας ἅτερος τὸν μὲν γὰρ ἕτερον εἴπομεν Δίχα που νυνδὴ διείλομεν τὴν ἄγραν πᾶσαν νευστικοῦ
greek_tokens: tis qhra ateros o men gar eteros eipon dixa pou nundi diairew o agra pas neustikos meros o de pezos temnw nai kai o men dierxomai osos peri o neustikos o enudros o de pezos eaw asxistos eipon ostis polueidhs eaw
english_head: What is the hunting of the second? We have spoken about the first. We just now divided hunting as a whole into two classes, and made one division that of swimming creatures and the other that of land-hunting. Yes. And the one we discussed, so far as the swimming creatures that live in the water are concerned; but we left the land-hunting undivided, merely remarking that it has many forms.

greek_head: Πάνυ γε Μέχρι μὲν τοίνυν ἐνταῦθα ὁ σοφιστὴς καὶ ὁ
greek_tokens: panu ge mexri men toinun entauqa o sofisths kai o aspalieuths ama apo o kthtikos texnh poreuw eoika goun ektrepw de ge apo o zwoqhrikos o men epi qalassa pou kai potamos kai limnh o en outos zwion qhreuw tis meis o de ge epi o gaia kai potamos eteros au tis ploutos kai neoths oios leimwn afqonos en outos qremma xeirow
english_head: Certainly. Now up to that point the sophist and the angler proceed together from the starting-point of acquisitive art. I think they do. But they separate at the point of animal-hunting, where the one turns to the sea and rivers and lakes to hunt the animals in those. To be sure. But the other turns toward the land and to rivers of a different kind—rivers of wealth and youth, bounteous meadows, as it were—and he intends to coerce the creatures in them.

greek_head: Πῶς λέγεις Τῆς πεζῆς θήρας γίγνεσθον δύο μεγίστω τινὲ
greek_tokens: pws legw o pezos qhra gignomai duo megas tis meros poios ekateros o men o hmeros o de o agrios eimi eimi tis qhra o hmeros eiper ge eimi anqrwpos hmeros zwion tiqhmi de oph xairw eite mhdeis tiqhmi hmeros eite allos men hmeros tis o de anqrwpos agrios eite hmeros men legw au o anqrwpos anqrwpos de mhdeis hgeomai qhra outos opoteros an hgeomai filos erw su outos egw diorizw
english_head: What do you mean? Of land-hunting there are two chief divisions. What are they? One is the hunting of tame, the other of wild creatures. Is there, then, a hunting of tame creatures? Yes, If man is a tame animal; but make any assumption you like, that there is no tame animal, or that some other tame animal exists but man is a wild one or that man is tame but there is no hunting of man. For the purpose of our definition choose whichever of these statements you think is satisfactory to you.

greek_head: Ἀλλ' ἡμᾶς τε ἥμερον ὦ ξένε ἡγοῦμαι ζῷον θήραν τε ἀνθρώπων εἶναι λέγω
greek_tokens: alla egw te hmeros eimi cenos hgeomai zwion qhra te anqrwpos eimi legos dissos toinun kai o hmeroqhrikos eipon kata tis legw o men lhstikos kai andrapodistikos kai turannikos kai sumpas o polemikos eis pas biaios qhra orizw kalos o de ge dikanikos kai dhmhgorikos kai prosomilhtikos eis au o sunolos piqanourgikos tis eis
english_head: Why, Stranger, I think we are a tame animal, and I agree that there is a hunting of man. Let us, then, say that the hunting of tame animals is also of two kinds. How do we justify that assertion? By defining piracy, man-stealing, tyranny, and the whole art of war all collectively as hunting by force. Excellent. And by giving the art of the law courts, of the public platform, and of conversation also a single name and calling

greek_head: τέχνην προσειπόντες Ὀρθῶς
greek_tokens: texnh proseipon orqos o dh piqanourgikos dissos legw genos poios o men eteros idios o de dhmosios gignomai gignomai gar oun eidos ekateros oukoun au o idioqhreutikos o men misqarnhtikos eimi o de dwroforikos ou manqanw o o eraw qhra o new ws eoika oupw prosexw o peri
english_head: them all collectively an art of persuasion. Correct. Now let us say that there are two kinds of persuasion. What kinds? The one has to do with private persons, the other with the community. Granted; each of them does form a class. Then again of the hunting of private persons one kind receives pay, and the other brings gifts, does it not? I do not understand. Apparently you have never yet paid attention to the lovers’ method of hunting. In what respect?

greek_head: Ὅτι τοῖς θηρευθεῖσι δῶρα προσεπιδιδόασιν Ἀληθέστατα λέγεις
greek_tokens: ostis o qhreuw dwron prosepididwmi alhqhs legw outos men toinun erwtikos texnh eimi eidos panu ge o de ge misqarnhtikos o men prosomilew dia xaris kai pantapasi dia hdona o delear poiew kai o misqos prassw trofh eautou monos kolakikos ws
english_head: That in addition to their other efforts they give presents to those whom they hunt. You are quite right. Let us, then, call this the amatory art. Agreed. But that part of the paid kind which converses to furnish gratification and makes pleasure exclusively its bait and demands as its pay only maintenance, we might all agree, if I am not mistaken,

greek_head: ἐγᾦμαι πάντες φαῖμεν ἂν ἢ ἡδυντικήν τινα τέχνην εἶναι Πῶς γὰρ οὔ
greek_tokens: oimh pas fhmi an h hduntikos tis texnh eimi pws gar ou o de epaggellw men ws aretaw eneka o omilia poiew misqos de nomisma prassw ara ou outos o genos eteros proseipon acios onoma pws gar ou tis dh outos peiraw legw dhlos dh o gar sofisths egw dokew aneuriskw outos oun egw eipon o proshkw onoma an hgeomai kalew autos
english_head: to call the art of flattery or of making things pleasant. Certainly. But the class which proposes to carry on its conversations for the sake of virtue and demands its pay in cash—does not this deserve to be called by another name? Of course. And what is that name? Try to tell. It is obvious; for I think we have discovered the sophist. And therefore by uttering that word I think I should give him the right name.

greek_head: Κατὰ δὴ τὸν νῦν ὦ Θεαίτητε λόγον ὡς ἔοικεν ἡ τέχνης οἰκειωτικῆς χειρωτικῆς κτητικῆς θηρευτικῆς
greek_tokens: kata dh o nun eimi qeaithtos logos ws eoika o texnh oikeiwtikos xeirwtikos kthtikos qhreutikos zwoqhria pezoqhria xersaios hmeroqhrikos anqrwpoqhria piqanoqhria idioqhria misqarnikos nomismatopwlikos docopaideutikos neos plousios kai endocos gignomai qhra prosrhteos ws o nun logos egw sumbainw sofistikos pantapasi men oun
english_head: Then, as it seems, according to our present reasoning, Theaetetus, the part of appropriative, coercive, hunting art which hunts animals, land animals, tame animals, man, privately, for pay, is paid in cash, claims to give education, and is a hunt after rich and promising youths, must—so our present argument concludes—be called sophistry. Most assuredly. But let us look at it in still another way; for the class we are now examining

greek_head: Ἔτι δὲ καὶ τῇδε ἴδωμεν οὐ γάρ τι φαύλης μέτοχόν ἐστι τέχνης τὸ νῦν ζητούμενον ἀλλ' εὖ μάλα ποικίλης καὶ
greek_tokens: eti de kai ode eidon ou gar tis faulos metoxos eimi texnh o nun zhtew alla eu mala poikilos kai gar oun en o prosqen erw fantasma parexw mh outos o nun autos egw fhmi alla eteros eimi tis genos ph dh o o kthtikos texnh diploos eimi eidos pou o men qhreutikos meros exw o de allaktikos eimi gar oun o toinun allaktikos duo eidos legw o men dwrhtikos o de eteros agorastikos erw kai meis au fhmi agorastikos dixaw temnw
english_head: partakes of no mean art, but of a very many-sided one. And we must indeed do so, for in our previous talk it presents an appearance of being, not what we now say it is, but another class. How so? The acquisitive art was of two sorts, the one the division of hunting, the other that of exchange. Yes, it was. Now shall we say that there are two sorts of exchange, the one by gift, the other by sale? So be it. And we shall say further that exchange by sale is divided into two parts.

greek_head: Πῇ Τὴν μὲν τῶν αὐτουργῶν αὐτοπωλικὴν διαιρουμένην
greek_tokens: ph o men o autourgew autopwlikos diairew o de o allotrios ergon metaballw metablhtikos panu ge tis de o metablhtikos ou o men kata polis allagh sxedon autos hmisus meros eimi kaphlikos prosagoreuw nai o de ge ek allos eis allos polis diallassw wneomai kai pipraskw emporikos tis de ou
english_head: How so? We make this distinction—calling the part which sells a man’s own productions the selling of one’s own, and the other, which exchanges the works of others, exchange. Certainly. Well, then, that part of exchange which is carried on in the city, amounting to about half of it, is called retailing, is it not? Yes. And that which exchanges goods from city to city by purchase and sale is called merchandising? Certainly. And have we not observed that one part

greek_head: Τῆς δ' ἐμπορικῆς ἆρ' οὐκ ᾐσθήμεθα ὅτι τὸ μὲν ὅσοις τὸ σῶμα τρέφεται καὶ χρῆται τὸ δὲ ὅσοις ἡ ψυχή πωλοῦν
greek_tokens: o de emporikos ara ou aisqanomai ostis o men osos o swma trefw kai xraomai o de osos o yuxh pwlew dia nomisma allassw pws outos legw o peri o yuxh isos agnoew epei o ge eteros pou sunihmi nai
english_head: of merchandising sells and exchanges for cash whatever serves the body for its support and needs, and the other whatever serves the soul? What do you mean by that? Perhaps we do not know about the part that has to do with the soul; though I fancy we do understand the other division. Yes.

greek_head: Μουσικήν τε τοίνυν συνάπασαν λέγωμεν ἐκ πόλεως ἑκάστοτε εἰς πόλιν ἔνθεν μὲν ὠνηθεῖσαν ἑτέρωσε δὲ ἀγομένην
greek_tokens: mousikh te toinun sunapas legw ek polis ekastote eis polis enqen men wneomai eterwse de agw kai pipraskw kai grafikos kai qaumatopoiikos kai polus eteros o yuxazw o men paramuqia o de kai spoudazw xaris agw kai pwleomai o agw kai pwlew mhdeis hsswn o o siteomai kai poths prasis emporos orqos an legw parexw alhqhs legw
english_head: Take, therefore, the liberal arts in general that constantly go about from city to city, bought in one place and carried to another and sold—painting, and conjuring, and the many other things that affect the soul, which are imported and sold partly for its entertainment and partly for its serious needs; we cannot deny that he who carries these about and sells them constitutes a merchant properly so called, no less than he whose business is the sale of food and drink. Very true.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν καὶ τὸν μαθήματα συνωνούμενον πόλιν τε ἐκ πόλεως νομίσματος ἀμείβοντα ταὐτὸν προσερεῖς ὄνομα
greek_tokens: oukoun kai o maqhma sunwneomai polis te ek polis nomisma ameibw tautos proserew onoma sfodra ge o dh yuxemporikos outos ara ou o men epideiktikos dikaios legw an o de geloios men ou hsswn o prosqen omws de maqhma eimi prasis autos adelfos tis o pracis onoma proseipon anagkh panu men oun outos toinun o maqhmatopwlikos o men peri
english_head: Then will you give the same name to him who buys up knowledge and goes about from city to city exchanging his wares for money? Certainly. One part of this soul-merchandising might very properly be called the art of display, might it not? But since the other part, though no less ridiculous than the first, is nevertheless a traffic in knowledge, must we not call it by some name akin to its business? Certainly. Now of this merchandising in knowledge

greek_head: τὰ τῶν ἄλλων τεχνῶν μαθήματα ἑτέρῳ τὸ δὲ περὶ τὸ τῆς ἀρετῆς ἄλλῳ προσρητέον
greek_tokens: o o allos texnh maqhma eteros o de peri o o aretaw allos prosrhteos pws gar ou texnopwlikos meis o ge peri allos an armozw o de peri outos su proqumeomai legw onoma kai tis tis an allos onoma eipon ou an plhmmelew plhn o nun zhtew autos eimi o sofistikos genos oudeis allos eimi dh nun sunagw autos legw ws o o kthtikos metablhtikos agorastikos
english_head: the part which has to do with the knowledge of the other arts should be called by one name, and that which has to do with virtue by another. Of course. The name of art-merchant would fit the one who trades in the other arts, and now do you be so good as to tell the name of him who trades in virtue. And what other name could one give, without making a mistake, than that which is the object of our present investigation—the sophist? No other. Come then, let us now summarize the matter by saying tha

greek_head: ἐμπορικῆς ψυχεμπορικῆς περὶ λόγους καὶ μαθήματα ἀρετῆς πωλητικὸν δεύτερον ἀνεφάνη σοφιστική
greek_tokens: emporikos yuxemporikos peri logos kai maqhma aretaw pwlhtikos deuteros anafainw sofistikos mala ge tritos de ge oimh su kan ei tis autos kaqidruw en polis o men wneomai o de kai tektainomai autos maqhma peri o autos outos kai pwlh ek outos o zaw protassw kalew oudeis allos plhn osper nundi tis de ou mellw
english_head: of trafficking, of merchandising, of soul-merchandising which deals in words and knowledge, and trades in virtue. Very well. But there is a third case: If a man settled down here in town and proposed to make his living by selling these same wares of knowledge, buying some of them and making others himself, you would, I fancy, not call him by any other name than that which you used a moment ago. Certainly not. Then also that part of acquisitive art which proceeds by exchange,

greek_head: Καὶ τὸ κτητικῆς ἄρα μεταβλητικόν ἀγοραστικόν καπηλικὸν εἴτε αὐτοπωλικόν ἀμφοτέρως ὅτιπερ ἂν ᾖ περὶ
greek_tokens: kai o kthtikos ara metablhtikos agorastikos kaphlikos eite autopwlikos amfoteros oti an eimi peri o toioutos maqhmatopwlikos genos aei su proserew ws fainw sofistikos anagkh o gar logos deomai sunakolouqew eti dh skopaw ei tis toiosde proseoika ara o nun metadiwkw genos
english_head: and by sale, whether as mere retail trade or the sale of one’s own productions, no matter which, so long as it is of the class of merchandising in knowledge, you will always, apparently, call sophistry. I must do so, for I have to follow where the argument leads. Let us examine further and see if the class we are now pursuing has still another aspect, of similar nature.

greek_head: Ποίῳ δή Τῆς κτητικῆς ἀγωνιστική τι μέρος ἡμῖν ἦν
greek_tokens: poios dh o kthtikos agwnistikos tis meros egw eimi eimi gar oun ou apo tropos toinun eimi diairew autos dixa kaqa opoios legos o men amillhtikos autos tiqhmi o de maxhtikos eimi o toinun maxhtikos o men swma pros swma gignomai sxedon eoika kai prepw onoma legw tis toioutos tiqhmi oios biastikos nai o de logos pros logos tis tis eimi qeaithtos allos
english_head: Of what nature? We agreed that fighting was a division of acquisitive art. Yes, we did. Then it is quite fitting to divide it into two parts. Tell what the parts are. Let us call one part of it the competitive and the other the pugnacious. Agreed. Then it is reasonable and fitting to give to that part of the pugnacious which consists of bodily contests some such name as violent. Yes. And what other name than controversy

greek_head: εἴπῃ πλὴν ἀμφισβητητικόν Οὐδέν
greek_tokens: eipon plhn amfisbhthtikos oudeis o de ge peri o amfisbhthsis qeteos dissos ph kaqa osos men gar gignomai mhkos te pros enantios mhkos logos kai peri o dikaios kai adikos dhmosios dikanikos nai o de en idios au kai katakermatizw erwthsis pros apokrisls mhs eqizw kalew allos plhn antilogikos oudeis o de antilogikos o men osos peri o sumbolaion
english_head: shall we give to the contests of words? No other. But controversy must be divided into two kinds. How? Whenever long speeches are opposed by long speeches on questions of justice and injustice in public, that is forensic controversy. Yes. But that which is carried on among private persons and is cut up into little bits by means of questions and their answers, we are accustomed to call argumentation, are we not? We are. And that part of argumentation which deals

greek_head: ἀμφισβητεῖται μέν εἰκῇ δὲ καὶ ἀτέχνως περὶ αὐτὸ πράττεται ταῦτα θετέον μὲν εἶδος ἐπείπερ αὐτὸ διέγνωκεν ὡς
greek_tokens: amfisbhtew men eikazw de kai atexnos peri autos prassw outos qeteos men eidos epei autos diagignwskw ws eteros eimi o logos atar epwnumios oute upo o emprosqen tugxanw oute nun ufh egw tugxanw acios alhqhs kata mikros gar lian kai pantodapos diairew o de ge entexnos kai peri dikaios autos kai adikos kai peri o allos oloc amfisbhtew ara ou eristikos au legw eqizw pws gar ou
english_head: with business contracts, in which there is controversy, to be sure, but it is carried on informally and without rules of art—all that must be considered a distinct class, now that our argument has recognized it as different from the rest, but it received no name from our predecessors, nor does it now deserve to receive one from us. True; for the divisions into which it falls are too small and too miscellaneous. But that which possesses rules of art and carries on controversy about abstract justi

greek_head: Τοῦ μὴν ἐριστικοῦ τὸ μὲν χρηματοφθορικόν τὸ δὲ χρηματιστικὸν ὂν τυγχάνει
greek_tokens: o meis eristikos o men xrhmatofqorikos o de xrhmatistikos eimi tugxanw pantapasi ge o epwnumios toinun os ekateros deomai kalew autos peiraw eipon oukoun xrh dokazw meis o ge dia hdona o peri outos diatribh amelhs o oikew gignomai peri de o lecis o polus o akouw ou meqo hdona akouw kalew kata gnwmh o emos ou eteros adolesxikos legw gar oun outws pws
english_head: Well, of disputation, one sort wastes money, the other makes money. Certainly. Then let us try to tell the name by which we must call each of these. Yes, we must do so. Presumably the kind which causes a man to neglect his own affairs for the pleasure of engaging in it, but the style of which causes no pleasure to most of his hearers, is, in my opinion, called by no other name than garrulity. Yes, that is about what it is called.

greek_head: Τούτου τοίνυν τοὐναντίον ἀπὸ τῶν ἰδιωτικῶν ἐρίδων χρηματιζόμενον ἐν τῷ μέρει σὺ πειρῶ νῦν εἰπεῖν
greek_tokens: outos toinun enantion apo o idiwtikos eris xrhmatizw en o meros su peiraw nun eipon kai tis tis an au eipon eteros ou ecamartanw plhn ge o qaumastos palin ekeinos hkw au nun tetartos o metadiwkw ufh egw sofisths
english_head: Then the opposite of this, the kind which makes money from private disputes—try now, for it is your turn, to give its name. What other answer could one give without making a mistake, than that now again for the fourth time that wonderful being whom we have so long been pursuing has turned up—the sophist!

greek_head: Οὐδὲν ἀλλ' ἢ τὸ χρηματιστικὸν γένος ὡς ἔοικεν ἐριστικῆς ὂν τέχνης τῆς ἀντιλογικῆς τῆς ἀμφισβητητικῆς
greek_tokens: oudeis alla h o xrhmatistikos genos ws eoika eristikos eimi texnh o antilogikos o amfisbhthtikos o maxhtikos o agwnistikos o kthtikos eimi ws o logos au mhnuw nun o sofisths komidh men oun oraw oun ws alhqhs legw o poikilos eimi outos o qhraw kai o legw ou o eteros lhptos oukoun amfw xrh
english_head: Yes, and the sophist is nothing else, apparently, than the money-making class of the disputatious, argumentative, controversial, pugnacious, combative, acquisitive art, as our argument has now again stated. Certainly. Do you see the truth of the statement that this creature is many-sided and, as the saying is, not to be caught with one hand? Then we must catch him with both. Yes, we must, and must go at it with all our might,

greek_head: Χρὴ γὰρ οὖν καὶ κατὰ δύναμίν γε οὕτω ποιητέον τοιόνδε τι μεταθέοντας ἴχνος αὐτοῦ καί μοι λέγε τῶν
greek_tokens: xrh gar oun kai kata dunamis ge outws poihteos toiosde tis metaqew ixnos autos kai egw legos o oiketikos onoma kalew atta pou kai polus atar poios dh o polus punqanomai o toiosde oios dihqew te legw kai diattaw kai brassw kai diakrinw tis meis kai pros ge outos eti cainw katagw kerkizw kai murios en o texnh allos toioutos eneimi efisthmi eimi gar
english_head: by following another track of his—in this way. Tell me; of the expressions connected with menial occupations some are in common use, are they not? Yes, many. But to which of the many does your question refer? To such as these: we say sift and strain and winnow and separate. Certainly. And besides these there are card and comb and beat the web and countless other technical terms which we know. Is it not so? Why do you use these as examples and ask about them all?

greek_head: Τὸ ποῖον αὐτῶν πέρι βουληθεὶς δηλῶσαι παραδείγματα προθεὶς ταῦτα κατὰ πάντων ἤρου
greek_tokens: o poios autos peri boulomai dhlow paradeigma protiqhmi outos kata pas arow diairetikos pou o legw eromai sumpas nai kata o emos toinun logos ws peri outos eis eimi en apas texnh eis onoma aciow autos tis proseipon diakritikos eimi skopaw dh outos au duo an ph dunamai kateidon eidos taxus ws egw skeyis epitassw
english_head: What do you wish to show in regard to them? All those that I have mentioned imply a notion of division. Yes. Then since there is, accorling to my reckoning, one art involved in all of these operations, let us give it one name. What shall we call it? The art of discrimination. Very well. Now see if we can discover two divisions of this. You demand quick thinking, for a boy like me.

greek_head: Καὶ μὴν ἔν γε ταῖς εἰρημέναις διακρίσεσι τὸ μὲν χεῖρον ἀπὸ βελτίονος ἀποχωρίζειν ἦν τὸ δ' ὅμοιον ἀφ'
greek_tokens: kai meis en ge o erw diakrisis o men xeirwn apo beltiwn apoxwrizw eimi o de omoios apo omoios sxedon outws nun legw fainw o men toinun onoma ou exw legw o de katalimpanw men o beltiwn diakrisis o de xeirwn apoballw exw legos tis pas o toioutos diakrisis ws egw sunnoew legw para pas kaqarmos tis legw gar oun
english_head: And yet, in the instance of discrimination just mentioned there was, first, the separation of worse from better, and, secondly, of like from like. Yes, as you now express it, that is pretty clear. Now I know no common name for the second kind of discrimination; but I do know the name of the kind which retains the better and throws away the worse. What is it? Every such discrimination, as I think, is universally called a sort of purification. Yes, so it is.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν τό γε καθαρτικὸν εἶδος αὖ διπλοῦν ὂν πᾶς ἂν ἴδοι
greek_tokens: oukoun o ge kaqartikos eidos au diploos eimi pas an eidon nai kata sxolh ge isos ou meis egw kaqoraw nun kai meis o ge peri o swma polus eidos kaqarsis eis perilambanw onoma proshkw poios kai tis o te o zwion osos entos swma upo gumnastikos
english_head: And could not anyone see that purification is of two kinds? Yes, perhaps, in time; but still I do not see it now. Still there are many kinds of purifications of bodies, and they may all properly be included under one name. What are they and what is the name? The purification of living creatures, having to do with impurities within the body, such as are successfully discriminated by gymnastics and medicine,

greek_head: ἰατρικῆς τε ὀρθῶς διακρινόμενα καθαίρεται καὶ περὶ τἀκτός εἰπεῖν μὲν φαῦλα ὅσα βαλανευτικὴ παρέχεται καὶ
greek_tokens: iatrikos te orqos diakrinw kaqairw kai peri ektos eipon men faulos osos balaneutikos parexw kai o ayuxos swma os gnafeutikos kai sumpas kosmhtikos o epimeleia parexw kata mikros polus kai geloios dokew onoma exw mala ge pantapasi men oun eimi qeaithtos alla gar o o logos meqodos spoggistikos h farmakoposia oudeis hsswn oude tis mallon tugxanw melw ei o men mikros o de megas egw wfelew kaqairw o ktaomai gar
english_head: and with those outside of the body, not nice to speak of, such as are attended to by the bath-keeper’s art; and the purification of inanimate bodies, which is the special care of the fuller’s art and in general of the art of exterior decoration; this, with its petty subdivisions, has taken on many names which seem ridiculous. Very. Certainly they do, Theaetetus. However, the method of argument is neither more nor less concerned with the art of medicine than with that of sponging, but is indiffer

greek_head: ἕνεκα νοῦν πασῶν τεχνῶν τὸ συγγενὲς καὶ τὸ μὴ συγγενὲς κατανοεῖν πειρωμένη τιμᾷ πρὸς τοῦτο ἐξ ἴσου πάσας καὶ
greek_tokens: eneka new pas texnh o suggenhs kai o mh suggenhs katanoew peiraw timaw pros outos ek isos passw kai eteros o eteros kata o omoioths oudeis hgeomai geloios semnos de tis o dia strathgikos h fqeiristikos dhlow qhreutikos oudeis nomizw alla ws o polus xaunos kai dh kai nun osper arow tis proserew onoma sumpas dunamis osos swma eite emyuxos eite ayuxos
english_head: It endeavors to understand what is related and what is not related in all arts, for the purpose of acquiring intelligence; and therefore it honors them all equally and does not in making comparisons think one more ridiculous than another, and does not consider him who employs, as his example of hunting, the art of generalship, any more dignified than him who employs the art of louse-catching, but only, for the most part, as more pretentious. And now as to your question, what name we shall give t

greek_head: εἰλήχασι καθαίρειν οὐδὲν αὐτῇ διοίσει ποῖόν τι λεχθὲν εὐπρεπέστατον εἶναι δόξει μόνον ἐχέτω χωρὶς τῶν τῆς
greek_tokens: lagxanw kaqairw oudeis autos diaferw poios tis legw euprephs eimi docis monos exw xwris o o yuxazw kaqarsis pas sundeomai osos allos tis kaqairw o gar peri o dianoia kaqarmos apo o allos epixeirew aforizw o nun ei ge osper boulomai manqanw alla manqanw kai sugxwrew duo men eidos kaqarsis eis de o peri o yuxh eidos eimi o peri o swma xwris eimi pas kallista kai egw o meta outos epakouw
english_head: what name sounds finest; it cares only to unite under one name all purifications of everything else and to keep them separate from the purification of the soul. For it has in our present discussion been trying to separate this purification definitely from the rest, if we understand its desire. But I do understand and I agree that there are two kinds of purification and that one kind is the purification of the soul, which is separate from that of the body. Most excellent. Now pay attention to the

greek_head: πειρώμενος αὖ τὸ λεχθὲν διχῇ τέμνειν Καθ' ὁποῖ' ἂν ὑφηγῇ πειράσομαί σοι συντέμνειν
greek_tokens: peiraw au o legw dixaw temnw kaqa opoios an ufhgeomai peiraw su suntemnw ponhria eteros aretaw en yuxw legw tis pws gar ou kai meis kaqarmos ge eimi o leipw men eteros ekballw de osos an eimi pou tis flauros eimi gar oun kai yuxazw ara kaqa osos an euriskw kakia afairesis tis kaqarmos autos legw en melos fqeggomai kai mala ge duo men eidos kakia peri yuxh rhteos poios
english_head: and try again to divide the term. In whatever way you suggest, I will try to help you in making the division. Do we say that wickedness is distinct from virtue in the soul? Of course. And purification was retaining the one and throwing out whatever is bad anywhere? Yes, it was. Hence whenever we find any removal of evil from the soul, we shall be speaking properly if we call that a purification. Very properly. We must say that there are two kinds of evil in the soul. What kinds?

greek_head: Τὸ μὲν οἷον νόσον ἐν σώματι τὸ δ' οἷον αἶσχος ἐγγιγνόμενον
greek_tokens: o men oios nosos en swma o de oios aisxos eggignomai ou manqanw nosos isos kai stasis ou tautos nomizw oude au pros outos exw tis xrh egw apokrinw poteros allos tis stasis hgeomai h o o fusis suggenhs ek tis diafqora diafora oudeis alla aisxos allos tis plhn o o ametrios pantaxou duseidhs eneimi genos
english_head: The one is comparable to a disease in the body, the other to a deformity. I do not understand. Perhaps you have not considered that disease and discord are the same thing? I do not know what reply I ought to make to this, either. Is that because you think discord is anything else than the disagreement of the naturally related, brought about by some corruption? No; I think it is nothing else. But is deformity anything else than the presence of the quality of disproportion, which is always ugly?

greek_head: Οὐδαμῶς ἄλλο Τί δέ ἐν ψυχῇ δόξας ἐπιθυμίαις καὶ θυμὸν ἡδοναῖς
greek_tokens: oudamos allos tis de en yuxw doca epiqumia kai qumos hdona kai logos luph kai pas allhlwn outos o flauros exw ou aisqanomai diaferw kai sfodra ge suggeneus ge meis ek anagkh sumpas gignomai pws gar ou stasis ara kai nosos o yuxazw ponhria legw orqos erew orqos men oun
english_head: Nothing else at all. Well then; do we not see that in the souls of worthless men opinions are opposed to desires, anger to pleasures, reason to pain, and all such things to one another? Yes, they are, decidedly. Yet they must all be naturally related. Of course. Then we shall be right if we say that wickedness is a discord and disease of the soul. Yes, quite right.

greek_head: Τί δ' ὅσ' ἂν κινήσεως μετασχόντα καὶ σκοπόν τινα θέμενα πειρώμενα τούτου τυγχάνειν καθ' ἑκάστην ὁρμὴν
greek_tokens: tis de osos an kinhsis metexw kai skopos tis tiqhmi peiraw outos tugxanw kaqa ekastos ormh paraforon autos gignomai kai apotugxanw poteros autos fhmi upo summetria o pros allhlwn h enantion upo ametrios autos pasxw dhlos ws upo ametrios alla meis yuxh ge oida aekwn pas pas agnoew sfodra ge o ge meis agnoew eimi epi alhqeia ormaw
english_head: But if things which partake of motion and aim at some particular mark pass beside the mark and miss it on every occasion when they try to hit it, shall we say that this happens to them through right proportion to one another or, on the contrary, through disproportion? Evidently through disproportion. But yet we know that every soul, if ignorant of anything, is ignorant against its will. Very much so. Now being ignorant is nothing else than

greek_head: ψυχῆς παραφόρου συνέσεως γιγνομένης οὐδὲν ἄλλο πλὴν παραφροσύνη
greek_tokens: yuxazw paraforon sunesis gignomai oudeis allos plhn parafrosunh panu men oun yuxh ara anohtos aisxros kai ametros qeteos eoika eimi dh duo outos ws fainw kakh en autos genos o men ponhria kalew upo o polus nosos autos safhs eimi nai o de ge agnoia men kalew kakia de autos en yuxw monos gignomai ou eqelw omologew
english_head: the aberration of a soul that aims at truth, when the understanding passes beside the mark. Very true. Then we must regard a foolish soul as deformed and ill-proportioned. So it seems. Then there are, it appears, these two kinds of evils in the soul, one, which people call wickedness, which is very clearly a disease. Yes. And the other they call ignorance, but they are not willing to acknowledge that it is vice, when it arises only in the soul.

greek_head: Κομιδῇ συγχωρητέον ὃ νυνδὴ λέξαντος ἠμφεγνόησά σου τὸ δύο εἶναι γένη κακίας ἐν ψυχῇ καὶ δειλίαν
greek_tokens: komidh sugxwrhteon o nundi legw amfignoew su o duo eimi genos kakia en yuxw kai deilia men kai akolasia kai adikia sumpas hghteon nosos en egw o de o polus kai pantodaphs agnoia paqos aisxos qeteos oukoun en ge swma peri duo paqhma outos duo texnh tis gignomai tinw outos
english_head: It must certainly be admitted, though I disputed it when you said it just now, that there are two kinds of vice in the soul, and that cowardice, intemperance, and injustice must all alike be considered a disease in us, and the widespread and various condition of ignorance must be regarded as a deformity. In the case of the body there are two arts which have to do with these two evil conditions, are there not? What are they?

greek_head: Περὶ μὲν αἶσχος γυμναστική περὶ δὲ νόσον ἰατρική Φαίνεσθον
greek_tokens: peri men aisxos gumnastikos peri de nosos iatrikos fainw oukoun kai peri men ubris kai adikia kai deilia o kolastikos fuw texnh malista dh pas prosakouw dikh o goun eoika ws eipon kata o anqrwpinos doca tis de peri sumpas agnoia mhs allos tis h didaskalikos orqos eipon tis an oudeis
english_head: For deformity there is gymnastics, and for disease medicine. That is clear. Hence for insolence and injustice and cowardice is not the corrective art the one of all arts most closely related to Justice? Probably it is, at least according to the judgement of mankind. And for all sorts of ignorance is there any art it would be more correct to suggest than that of instruction? No, none. Come now, think. Shall we say that

greek_head: Φέρε δή διδασκαλικῆς δὲ ἆρα ἓν μόνον γένος φατέον εἶναι ἢ πλείω δύο δέ τινε αὐτῆς εἶναι μεγίστω
greek_tokens: ferw dh didaskalikos de ara eis monos genos fateon eimi h plew duo de tis autos eimi megas skopaw skopaw kai egw dokew ode an ph taxistos euriskw ph o agnoia eidon ei ph kata mesos eautou tomh exis tis diploos gar outos gignomai dhlos ostis kai o didaskalikos duo anagkazw morion exw eis epi eis genos o eautou ekateros tis oun katafanhs ph su o nun zhtew
english_head: there is only one kind of instruction, or that there are more and that two are the most important? I am thinking. I think we can find out most quickly in this way. In what way? By seeing whether ignorance admits of being cut in two in the middle; for if ignorance turns out to be twofold, it is clear that instruction must also consist of two parts, one for each part of ignorance. Well, can you see what you are now looking for?

greek_head: Ἀγνοίας γοῦν μέγα τί μοι δοκῶ καὶ χαλεπὸν ἀφωρισμένον ὁρᾶν εἶδος πᾶσι τοῖς ἄλλοις αὐτῆς ἀντίσταθμον
greek_tokens: agnoia goun megas tis egw dokazw kai xalepos aforizw oraw eidos pas o allos autos antistaqmos meros poios dh o mh katoida tis dokew oida dia e kinduneuw pas osos dianoia sfallw gignomai pas alhqhs kai dh kai outos ge oimh monos o agnoia amaqia onoma proserew panu ge tis de dh o o didaskalikos ara meros o outos apallassw lekteos
english_head: I at any rate think I do see one large and grievous kind of ignorance, separate from the rest, and as weighty as all the other parts put together. What is it? Thinking that one knows a thing when one does not know it. Through this, I believe, all the mistakes of the mind are caused in all of us. True. And furthermore to this kind of ignorance alone the name of stupidity is given. Certainly. Now what name is to be given to that part of instruction which gets rid of this?

greek_head: Οἶμαι μὲν οὖν ὦ ξένε τὸ μὲν ἄλλο δημιουργικὰς διδασκαλίας τοῦτο δὲ ἐνθάδε γε παιδείαν δι' ἡμῶν
greek_tokens: oimh men oun eimi cenos o men allos dhmiourgikos didaskalia outos de enqade ge paideia dia egw kalew kai gar sxedon eimi qeaithtos en pas ellhn alla gar egw eti kai outos skepteos ara atomos hdh eimi pas h tis exw diairesis acios epwnumios oukoun xrh skopaw dokew toinun egw kai outos eti ph sxizw kata tis
english_head: I think, Stranger, that the other part is called instruction in handicraft, and that this part is here at Athens through our influence called education. And so it is, Theaetetus, among nearly all the Hellenes. But we must examine further and see whether it is one and indivisible or still admits of division important enough to have a name. Yes, we must see about that. I think there is still a way in which this also may be divided. On what principle? Of instruction in arguments one method

greek_head: Τῆς ἐν τοῖς λόγοις διδασκαλικῆς ἡ μὲν τραχυτέρα τις ἔοικεν ὁδὸς εἶναι τὸ δ' ἕτερον αὐτῆς μόριον λειότερον
greek_tokens: o en o logos didaskalikos o men traxus tis eoika odos eimi o de eteros autos morion leios o poios dh outos ekateros legw o men arxaioprephs tis patrios os pros o uios malista xraomai te kai eti polus xraomai o nun otan
english_head: seems to be rougher, and the other section smoother. What shall we call each of these? The venerable method of our fathers, which they generally employed towards their sons, and which many still employ, of sometimes showing anger at their errors

greek_head: αὐτοῖς ἐξαμαρτάνωσί τι τὰ μὲν χαλεπαίνοντες τὰ δὲ μαλθακωτέρως παραμυθούμενοι τὸ δ' οὖν σύμπαν αὐτὸ ὀρθότατα
greek_tokens: autos ecamartanw tis o men xalepainw o de malqakos paramuqeomai o de oun sumpas autos orqos eipon tis an nouqethtikos eimi outws o de ge eoika tis au logos eautou didwmi hgeomai pas aekousios amaqia eimi kai manqanw oudeis pote an eqelw o oiomai eimi sofos outos os oiomai peri deinos eimi meta de polus ponos o nouqethtikos eidos o paideia mikros anuw orqos ge nomizw
english_head: and sometimes more gently exhorting them—that would most properly be called as a whole admonition. That is true. On the other hand, some appear to have convinced themselves that all ignorance is involuntary, and that he who thinks himself wise would never be willing to learn any of those things in which he believes he is clever, and that the admonitory kind of education takes a deal of trouble and accomplishes little. They are quite right.

greek_head: Τῷ τοι ταύτης τῆς δόξης ἐπὶ ἐκβολὴν ἄλλῳ τρόπῳ στέλλονται
greek_tokens: o su outos o doca epi ekbolh allos tropos stellw tis dh dierwtaw os an oiomai tis tis peri legw legos mhdeis eimi ate planaw o doca radios ecetazw kai sunagw dh o logos eis tautos tiqhmi para allhlwn tiqhmi de epideiknumi autos eautou ama peri o autos pros o autos kata tautos enantios e de oraw eautou men xalepainw pros de o allos hmerow
english_head: So they set themselves to cast out the conceit of cleverness in another way. In what way? They question a man about the things about which he thinks he is talking sense when he is talking nonsense; then they easily discover that his opinions are like those of men who wander, and in their discussions they collect those opinions and compare them with one another, and by the comparison they show that they contradict one another about the same things, in relation to the same things and in respect to

greek_head: καὶ τούτῳ δὴ τῷ τρόπῳ τῶν περὶ αὑτοὺς μεγάλων καὶ σκληρῶν δοξῶν ἀπαλλάττονται πασῶν τε ἀπαλλαγῶν ἀκούειν τε
greek_tokens: kai outos dh o tropos o peri eautou megas kai sklhros doca apallassw pas te apallagh akouw te hdus kai o pasxw bebaios gignomai nomizw gar eimi pais filos e kaqairw autos wsper e peri o swma iatros nomizw mh proteros an o prosferw trefw apolauw dunamai swma prin an o empodizw entos tis ekballw tautos kai peri yuxazw dianoeomai ekeinos mh proteros autos exw o prosferw maqhma
english_head: they are freed from their high and obstinate opinions about themselves. The process of freeing them, moreover, affords the greatest pleasure to the listeners and the most lasting benefit to him who is subjected to it. For just as physicians who care for the body believe that the body cannot get benefit from any food offered to it until all obstructions are removed, so, my boy, those who purge the soul believe that the soul can receive no benefit from any teachings offered to it

greek_head: ὄνησιν πρὶν ἂν ἐλέγχων τις τὸν ἐλεγχόμενον εἰς αἰσχύνην καταστήσας τὰς τοῖς μαθήμασιν ἐμποδίους δόξας ἐξελών
greek_tokens: onhsis prin an elegxos tis o elegxw eis aisxunh kaqisthmi o o maqhma empodios doca ecairew kaqaros apofainw kai outos hgeomai o oida oida monos plew de mh beltistos goun kai swfrwn o ecis outos dia outos dh pas egw eimi qeaithtos kai o elegxos lekteos ws ara megas kai kurios o kaqarsis eimi kai o anelegktos au nomisteos an kai tugxanw
english_head: until someone by cross-questioning reduces him who is cross-questioned to an attitude of modesty, by removing the opinions that obstruct the teachings, and thus purges him and makes him think that he knows only what he knows, and no more. That is surely the best and most reasonable state of mind. For all these reasons, Theaetetus, we must assert that cross-questioning is the greatest and most efficacious of all purifications, and that he who is not cross-questioned, even though he be the Great K

greek_head: βασιλεὺς ὁ μέγας ὤν τὰ μέγιστα ἀκάθαρτον ὄντα ἀπαίδευτόν τε καὶ αἰσχρὸν γεγονέναι ταῦτα ἃ καθαρώτατον καὶ κάλλιστον
greek_tokens: basileus o megas eimi o megas akaqartos eimi apaideutos te kai aisxros gignomai outos o kaqaros kai kalos prepw o ontws eimi eudaimwn eimi pantapasi men oun tis de o outos xraomai o texnh tis
english_head: has not been purified of the greatest taints, and is therefore uneducated and deformed in those things in which he who is to be truly happy ought to be most pure and beautiful. Perfectly true. Well then, who are those who practise this art?

greek_head: φήσομεν ἐγὼ μὲν γὰρ φοβοῦμαι σοφιστὰς φάναι Τί δή
greek_tokens: fhmi egw men gar fobew sofisths fainw tis dh mh megas autos prosaptw geras alla meis proseoika ge toioutos tis o nun erw kai gar kuwn lukos agrios hmeros o de asfalhs deomai pas malista peri o omoioths aei poiew o fulakh olisqheis gar o genos omws de eimi ou gar peri mikros oros o amfisbhthsis oiomai
english_head: I am afraid to say the sophists. Why so? Lest we grant them too high a meed of honor. But the description you have just given is very like someone of that sort. Yes, and a wolf is very like a dog, the wildest like the tamest of animals. But the cautious man must be especially on his guard in the matter of resemblances, for they are very slippery things. However, let us agree that they are the sophists; for I think the strife will not be about petty discriminations

greek_head: γενήσεσθαι τότε ὁπόταν ἱκανῶς φυλάττωσιν Οὔκουν τό γε εἰκός
greek_tokens: gignomai tote opotan ikanos fulassw oukoun o ge eoika eimi dh diakritikos texnh kaqartikos kaqartikos de o peri yuxh meros aforizw outos de didaskalikos didaskalikos de paideutikos o de paideutikos o peri o mataios docosofia gignomai elegxos en o nun logos parafainw mhdeis allos egw eimi legw plhn o genos gennaios sofistikos legw men aforaw de egw hdh dia o polus
english_head: when people are sufficiently on their guard. No, probably not. Then let it be agreed that part of the discriminating art is purification, and as part of purification let that which is concerned with the soul be separated off, and as part of this, instruction, and as part of instruction, education; and let us agree that the cross-questioning of empty conceit of wisdom, which has come to light in our present discussion, is nothing else than the true-born art of sophistry. Let us agree to all that;

greek_head: πεφάνθαι τί χρή ποτε ὡς ἀληθῆ λέγοντα καὶ διισχυριζόμενον εἰπεῖν ὄντως εἶναι τὸν σοφιστήν
greek_tokens: fainw tis xrh pote ws alhqhs legw kai diisxurizomai eipon ontws eimi o sofisths eoikotws ge su aforaw alla su ekeinos hgeomai xrh nun hdh sfodra aforaw oph pote eti diadunw o logos orqos gar o paroimia o o apas mh radios eimi diafeugw nun oun kai malista epiqeteon autos kalos legw proteros dh isthmi oios anaaponew kai pros
english_head: to know what in the world to say he really is, with any assurance that I am speaking the truth. No wonder you are at a loss. But it is fair to suppose that by this time he is still more at a loss to know how he can any longer elude our argument; for the proverb is right which says it is not easy to escape all the wrestler’s grips. So now we must attack him with redoubled vigor. You are right. First, then, let us stop to take breath and while we are resting let us count up

greek_head: ἡμᾶς αὐτοὺς διαλογισώμεθα ἅμα ἀναπαυόμενοι φέρε ὁπόσα ἡμῖν ὁ σοφιστὴς πέφανται δοκῶ μὲν γάρ τὸ πρῶτον
greek_tokens: egw autos dialogizomai ama anapauw ferw oposos egw o sofisths fainw dokazw men gar o proteros euriskw neos kai plousios emmisqos qhreuths nai o de ge deuteros emporos tis peri o o yuxazw maqhma panu ge tritos de ara ou peri autos outos kaphlos anafainw nai kai tetartos ge autopwlhs peri o maqhma egw eimi orqos mnhmoneuw pemptos de egw peiraw
english_head: the number of forms in which the sophist has appeared to us. First, I believe, he was found to be a paid hunter after the young and wealthy. Yes. And secondly a kind of merchant in articles of knowledge for the soul. Certainly. And thirdly did he not turn up as a retailer of these same articles of knowledge? Yes, and fourthly we found he was a seller of his own productions of knowledge. Your memory is good; but I will try to recall the fifth case myself. He was an athlete

greek_head: μνημονεύειν τῆς γὰρ ἀγωνιστικῆς περὶ λόγους ἦν τις ἀθλητής τὴν ἐριστικὴν τέχνην ἀφωρισμένος
greek_tokens: mnhmoneuw o gar agwnistikos peri logos eimi tis aqlhths o eristikos texnh aforizw eimi gar oun o ge meis ektos amfisbhthsimos men omws de eqemen autos sugxwrew doca empodios maqhma peri yuxh kaqarths autos eimi pantapasi men oun
english_head: in contests of words, who had taken for his own the art of disputation. Yes, he was. The sixth case was doubtful, but nevertheless we agreed to consider him a purger of souls, who removes opinions that obstruct learning. Very true.

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν ἐννοεῖς ὅταν ἐπιστήμων τις πολλῶν φαίνηται μιᾶς δὲ τέχνης ὀνόματι προσαγορεύηται τὸ φάντασμα
greek_tokens: ara oun ennoew otan episthmos tis polus fainw eis de texnh onoma prosagoreuw o fantasma outos ws ou esqw ugihs alla dhlos ws o pasxw autos pros tis texnh ou dunamai kateidon ekeinos autos eis o pas o maqhma outos blepos dio kai polus onoma anti eis o exw autos prosagoreuw kinduneuw outos outos ph malista fuw
english_head: Then do you see that when a man appears to know many things, but is called by the name of a single art, there is something wrong about this impression, and that, in fact, the person who labors under this impression in connexion with any art is clearly unable to see the common principle of the art, to which all these kinds of knowledge pertain, so that he calls him who possesses them by many names instead of one? Something like that is very likely to be the case.

greek_head: Μὴ τοίνυν ἡμεῖς γε αὐτὸ ἐν τῇ ζητήσει δι' ἀργίαν πάσχωμεν ἀλλ' ἀναλάβωμεν ἓν πρῶτον τῶν περὶ τὸν
greek_tokens: mh toinun egw ge autos en o zhthsis dia argia pasxw alla analambanw eis proteros o peri o sofisths erw eis gar tis egw malista katafainw autos mhnuw o poios antilogikos autos fhmi eimi pou nai tis de ou kai o allos autos outos didaskalos gignomai tis meis skopaw dh peri tis ara kai fhmi e toioutos poiew antilogikos o de skeyis egw ek arxh eimi ode
english_head: We must not let that happen to us in our search through lack of diligence. So let us first take up again one of our statements about the sophist. For there is one of them which seemed to me to designate him most plainly. Which was it? I think we said he was a disputer. Yes. And did we not also say that he taught this same art of disputing to others? Certainly. Now let us examine and see what the subjects are about which such men say they make their pupils able to dispute. Let us begin our examin

greek_head: πῃ φέρε περὶ τῶν θείων ὅσ' ἀφανῆ τοῖς πολλοῖς ἆρ' ἱκανοὺς ποιοῦσι τοῦτο δρᾶν
greek_tokens: ph ferw peri o qew osos afanhs o polus ara ikanos poiew outos draw legw goun dh peri autos outos tis de osos faneros gaia te kai ouranos kai o peri o toioutos tis gar alla meis en ge o idios sunousia opotan genesis te kai ousia peri kata pas legw tis sunoida ws autos te anteipon deinos o te allos ostis poiew o autos dunatos pantapasi ge
english_head: at the beginning with this question: Is it about divine things which are invisible to others that they make people able to dispute? That is their reputation, at any rate. And how about the visible things of earth and heaven and the like? Those are included, of course. And furthermore in private conversations, when the talk is about generation and being in general, we know (do we not?) that they are clever disputants themselves and impart equal ability to others. Certainly.

greek_head: Τί δ' αὖ περὶ νόμων καὶ συμπάντων τῶν πολιτικῶν ἆρ' οὐχ ὑπισχνοῦνται ποιεῖν ἀμφισβητητικούς
greek_tokens: tis de au peri nomos kai sumpas o politikos ara ou upisxneomai poiew amfisbhthtikos oudeis gar an autos ws epos eipon dialegw mh outos upisxneomai o ge meis peri pas te kai kata eis ekastos texnh o deomai pros ekastos autos o dhmiourgos anteipon dhmosiow pou kataballw grafw o boulomai manqanw o prwtagoreios egw fainw peri te pala kai
english_head: And how about laws and public affairs in general? Do they not promise to make men able to argue about those? Yes, for nobody, to speak broadly, would attend their classes if they did not make that promise. However in all arts jointly and severally what the professional ought to answer to every opponent is written down somewhere and published that he who will may learn. You seem to refer to the text-books of Protagoras

greek_head: τῶν ἄλλων τεχνῶν εἰρηκέναι Καὶ πολλῶν γε ὦ μακάριε ἑτέρων ἀτὰρ δὴ τὸ
greek_tokens: o allos texnh erw kai polus ge eimi makarios eteros atar dh o o antilogikos texnh ara ou en kefalaios peri pas pros amfisbhthsis ikanos tis dunamis eoika eimi fainw goun dh sxedon oudeis upoleipw su dh pros qea eimi pais dunatos hgeomai outos taxa gar an su men ocus e neos pros autos blepw egw de amblus
english_head: on wrestling and the other arts. Yes, my friend, and to those of many other authors. But is not the art of disputation, in a word, a trained ability for arguing about all things? Well, at any rate, it does not seem to leave much out. For heaven’s sake, my boy, do you think that is possible? For perhaps you young people may look at the matter with sharper vision than our duller sight.

greek_head: Τὸ ποῖον καὶ πρὸς τί μάλιστα λέγεις οὐ γάρ που κατανοῶ τὸ νῦν ἐρωτώμενον
greek_tokens: o poios kai pros tis malista legw ou gar pou katanoew o nun erwtaw ei pas efisthmi tis anqrwpos eimi dunatos makarios mentoi egw eimi cenos eimi o genos pws oun an pote tis pros ge o efisthmi autos anepisthmwn eimi dunamai an ugihs tis legos anteipon oudamos tis pote oun an eaw o o sofistikos dunamis qauma o dh peri
english_head: What do you mean and just what do you refer to? I do not yet understand your question. I ask whether it is possible for a man to know all things. If that were possible, Stranger, ours would indeed be a blessed race. How, then, can one who is himself ignorant say anything worth while in arguing with one who knows? He cannot at all. Then what in the world can the magical power of the sophistical art be? Magical power in what respect?

greek_head: Καθ' ὅντινα τρόπον ποτὲ δυνατοὶ τοῖς νέοις δόξαν παρασκευάζειν ὡς εἰσὶ πάντα πάντων αὐτοὶ σοφώτατοι
greek_tokens: kaqa ostis tropos pote dunatos o neos doca paraskeuazw ws eimi pas pas autos sofos dhlos gar ws ei mhte antilegw orqos mhte ekeinos fainw fainw te ei mhdeis au mallon dokew dia o amfisbhthsis eimi fronimos o sos dh outos sxolazw pote an autos tis xrhma didwmi eqelw an outos autos maqhths gignomai sxolazw mentoi nun de ge eqelw kai mala
english_head: In the way in which they are able to make young men think that they themselves are in all matters the wisest of men. For it is clear that if they neither disputed correctly nor seemed to the young men to do so, or again if they did seem to dispute rightly but were not considered wiser on that account, nobody, to quote from you, would care to pay them money to become their pupil in these subjects. Certainly not. But now people do care to do so? Very much.

greek_head: Δοκοῦσι γὰρ οἶμαι πρὸς ταῦτα ἐπιστημόνως ἔχειν αὐτοὶ πρὸς ἅπερ ἀντιλέγουσιν
greek_tokens: dokew gar oimh pros outos episthmwn exw autos pros o antilegw pws gar ou draw de ge outos pros apas fhmi nai pas ara sofos o maqhths fainw tis meis ou eimi ge adunatos gar outos ge fainw pws gar ou adunatos docastikos ara tis peri pas episthmh o sofisths egw alla ou alhqeia exw anafainw
english_head: Yes, for they are supposed, I fancy, to have knowledge themselves of the things about which they dispute. Of course. And they do that about all things, do they not? Yes. Then they appear to their pupils to be wise in all things. To be sure. Though they are not; for that was shown to be impossible. Of course it is impossible. Then it is a sort of knowledge based upon mere opinion that the sophist has been shown to possess about all things, not true knowledge.

greek_head: Παντάπασι μὲν οὖν καὶ κινδυνεύει γε τὸ νῦν εἰρημένον ὀρθότατα περὶ αὐτῶν εἰρῆσθαι
greek_tokens: pantapasi men oun kai kinduneuw ge o nun erw orqos peri autos erw lambanw toinun safhs tis paradeigma peri outos o poios dh ode kai egw peiraw prosexw o new eu mala apokrinw o poios ei tis fhmi mh legw mhde antilegw alla poiew kai draw eis texnh sunapas efisthmi pragma
english_head: Certainly; and I shouldn’t be surprised if that were the most accurate statement we have made about him so far. Let us then take a clearer example to explain this. What sort of an example? This one; and try to pay attention and to give a very careful answer to my question. What is the question? If anyone should say that by virtue of a single art he knew how, not to assert or dispute, but to do and make all things—

greek_head: Πῶς πάντα εἶπες Τὴν ἀρχὴν τοῦ ῥηθέντος σύ γ' ἡμῖν εὐθὺς ἀγνοεῖς
greek_tokens: pws pas eipon o arxh o erw su ge egw euqus agnoew o gar sumpas ws eoika ou manqanw ou gar oun legos toinun sos kai egw o pas kai pros egw allos zwion kai dendron pws legw ei tis egw kai sos kai allos futas pas poiew fhmi
english_head: What do you mean by all things? You fail to grasp the very beginning of what I said; for apparently you do not understand the word all. No, I do not. I mean you and me among the all, and the other animals besides, and the trees. What do you mean? If one should say that he would make you and me and all other created beings. What would he mean by making? Evidently you will not say

greek_head: Τίνα δὴ λέγων τὴν ποίησιν οὐ γὰρ δὴ γεωργόν γε ἐρεῖς τινα καὶ γὰρ ζῴων αὐτὸν εἶπες ποιητήν
greek_tokens: tis dh legos o poihsis ou gar dh gewrgos ge erew tis kai gar zwion autos eipon poihths fhmi kai pros ge qalassa kai gaia kai ouranos kai qea kai o allos sumpas kai toinun kai taxus poiew autos ekastos panu mikros nomisma apodidwmi paidih legw tis tis de o o legw ostis pas oida kai outos eteros an didaskw oligos kai en oligos xronos mhs ou paidih nomisteos pantws pou
english_head: that he means a husbandman; for you said he was a maker of animals also. Yes, and of sea and earth and heaven and gods and everything else besides; and, moreover, he makes them all quickly and sells them for very little. This is some joke of yours. Yes? And when a man says that he knows all things and can teach them to another for a small price in a little time, must we not consider that a joke? Surely we must.

greek_head: Παιδιᾶς δὲ ἔχεις ἤ τι τεχνικώτερον ἢ καὶ χαριέστερον εἶδος ἢ τὸ μιμητικόν
greek_tokens: paidih de exis h tis texnikos h kai xarieis eidos h o mimhtikos oudamos pampolus gar erw eidos eis eis pas sullambanw kai sxedon poikilos oukoun o ge upisxneomai dunatos eimi eis texnh pas poiew gignwskw pou outos ostis mimhma kai omwnumos o eimi apergazomai o grafikos texnh dunatos eimi o anohtos o neos pais proswqen o grafw epideiknumi lanqanw ws oti an boulomai draw outos ikanos eimi apotelew ergon
english_head: And is there any more artistic or charming kind of joke than the imitative kind? Certainly not; for it is of very frequent occurrence and, if I may say so, most diverse. Your expression is very comprehensive. And so we recognize that he who professes to be able by virtue of a single art to make all things will be able by virtue of the painter’s art, to make imitations which have the same names as the real things, and by showing the pictures at a distance will be able to deceive the duller ones a

greek_head: Πῶς γὰρ οὔ Τί δὲ δή περὶ τοὺς λόγους ἆρ' οὐ προσδοκῶμεν
greek_tokens: pws gar ou tis de dh peri o logos ara ou prosdokaw eimi tis allos texnh h au dunatos eimi au tugxanw o neos kai eti prosw o pragma o alhqeia afisthmi dia o aatos o logos gohteuw deiknumi eidwlon legw peri pas wste poiew alhqhs dokew legw kai o legw dh sofos pas apas eimi
english_head: Certainly. Well then, may we not expect to find that there is another art which has to do with words, by virtue of which it is possible to bewitch the young through their ears with words while they are still standing at a distance from the realities of truth, by exhibiting to them spoken images of all things, so as to make it seem that they are true and that the speaker is the wisest of all men in all things?
