greek_head: Τί γὰρ οὐκ ἂν εἴη ἄλλη τις τοιαύτη τέχνη Τοὺς πολλοὺς οὖν ὦ Θεαίτητε τῶν τότε ἀκουόντων
greek_tokens: tis gar ou an eaw allos tis toioutos texnh o polus oun eimi qeaithtos o tote akouw ara ou anagkh xronos te eperxomai autos ikanos kai proeimi hlikia o te eimi prospitnw egguqen kai dia paqhma anagkazw enarghs efaptw o eimi metaballw o tote gignomai doca wste mikros men fainw o megas xalepos de o radia kai pas
english_head: Why should there not be such another art? Now most of the hearers, Theaetetus, when they have lived longer and grown older, will perforce come closer to realities and will be forced by sad experience openly to lay hold on realities; they will have to change the opinions which they had at first accepted, so that what was great will appear small and what was easy, difficult, and

greek_head: πάντῃ ἀνατετράφθαι τὰ ἐν τοῖς λόγοις φαντάσματα ὑπὸ τῶν ἐν ταῖς πράξεσιν ἔργων παραγενομένων
greek_tokens: panth anatrepw o en o logos fantasma upo o en o pracis ergnumi paragignomai ws goun egw thlikosde eimi krinw oimh de kai egw o eti proswqen afisthmi eimi toigar egw su ode pas peiraw kai nun peirw ws eggus aneu o paqhma prosagw peri de oun o sofisths ode egw legos poteros hdh outos
english_head: all the apparent truths in arguments will be turned topsy-turvy by the facts that have come upon them in real life. Is not this true? Yes, at least so far as one of my age can judge. But I imagine I am one of those who are still standing at a distance. Therefore all of us elders here will try, and are now trying, to bring you as near as possible without the sad experience. So answer this question about the sophist:

greek_head: σαφές ὅτι τῶν γοήτων ἐστί τις μιμητὴς ὢν τῶν ὄντων ἢ διστάζομεν ἔτι μὴ περὶ ὅσωνπερ ἀντιλέγειν δοκεῖ δυνατὸς
greek_tokens: safhs ostis o gohs eimi tis mimhths eimi o eimi h distazw eti mh peri osos antilegw dokew dunatos eimi peri tosoutos kai o episthmh alhqhs exw tugxanw kai pws an eimi cenos alla sxedon hdh safhs ek o erw ostis o o paidih metexw eimi tis mera eis gohs men dh kai mimhths ara qeteos autos tis pws gar ou qeteos age dh nun hmeteros ergnumi hdh o qhr mhketi
english_head: Is this now clear, that he is a kind of a juggler, an imitator of realities, or are we still uncertain whether he may not truly possess the knowledge of all the things about which he seems to be able to argue? How could that be, my dear sir? Surely it is pretty clear by this time from what has been said that he is one of those whose business is entertainment. That is to say, he must be classed as a juggler and imitator. Of course he must. Look sharp, then; it is now our business not to let

greek_head: ἀνεῖναι σχεδὸν γὰρ αὐτὸν περιειλήφαμεν ἐν ἀμφιβληστρικῷ τινι τῶν ἐν τοῖς λόγοις περὶ τὰ τοιαῦτα ὀργάνων ὥστε
greek_tokens: anihmi sxedon gar autos perilambanw en amfiblhstrikos tis o en o logos peri o toioutos organon wste ouketi ekfeugw ode ge o poios o mh ou o genos eimi o o qaumatopoiew tis eis amos outos ge outws peri autos sundokew dokew toinun ostis taxistos diairew o eidwlopoiikos texnh kai katabainw eis autos ean men egw euqus o sofisths upomenw sullambanw autos kata o epistellw
english_head: the beast get away again, for we have almost got him into a kind of encircling net of the devices we employ in arguments about such subjects, so that he will not now escape the next thing. What next thing? The conclusion that he belongs to the class of conjurers. I agree to that opinion of him, too. It is decided, then, that we will as quickly as possible divide the image-making art and go down into it, and if the sophist stands his ground against us at first, we will seize him by the orders of

greek_head: ὑπὸ τοῦ βασιλικοῦ λόγου κἀκείνῳ παραδόντας ἀποφῆναι τὴν ἄγραν ἐὰν δ' ἄρα κατὰ μέρη τῆς μιμητικῆς δύηταί πῃ
greek_tokens: upo o basilikos logos ekeinos paradidwmi apofainw o agra ean de ara kata mera o mimhtikos duw ph sunakolouqew autos diairew aei o upodexomai autos moira ewsper an lambanw pantws oute outos oute allos genos oudeis mh pote ekfeugw epeuxomai o o outws dunamai meteimi kaqa ekastos te kai epi pas meqodos legw eu kai outos outos poihteos kata dh o parerxomai tropos o diairesis
english_head: our king, then deliver him up to the king and display his capture. But if he tries to take cover in any of the various sections of the imitative art, we must follow him, always dividing the section into which he has retreated, until he is caught. For assuredly neither he nor any other creature will ever boast of having escaped from pursuers who are able to follow up the pursuit in detail and everywhere in this methodical way. You are right. That is what we must do. To return, then, to our previo

greek_head: ἔγωγέ μοι καὶ νῦν φαίνομαι δύο καθορᾶν εἴδη τῆς μιμητικῆς τὴν δὲ ζητουμένην ἰδέαν ἐν ὁποτέρῳ ποθ' ἡμῖν οὖσα τυγχάνει
greek_tokens: egw egw kai nun fainw duo kaqoraw eidos o mimhtikos o de zhtew idea en opoteros poqh egw eimi tugxanw katamanqanw oudepw egw dokazw nun dunatos eimi su de alla eipon proteros kai diairew egw tinw o duo legw eis men o eikastikos oraw en autos texnh eimi de outos malista opotan kata o o paradeigma summetria tis en mhkos kai platos kai baqos kai pros
english_head: I think I see this time also two classes of imitation, but I do not yet seem to be able to make out in which of them the form we are seeking is to be found. Please first make the division and tell us what two classes you mean. I see the likeness-making art as one part of imitation. This is met with, as a rule, whenever anyone produces the imitation by following the proportions of the original in length, breadth, and depth, and giving, besides,

greek_head: τούτοις ἔτι χρώματα ἀποδιδοὺς τὰ προσήκοντα ἑκάστοις τὴν τοῦ μιμήματος γένεσιν ἀπεργάζηται
greek_tokens: outos eti xrwma apodidwmi o proshkw ekastos o o mimhma genesis apergazomai tis de ou pas e mimeomai tis outos epixeirew draw oukoun osos ge o megas pou tis plassw ergnumi h grafw ei gar apodidwmi o o kalh alhqinos summetria oistos ostis mikros men o deon
english_head: the appropriate colors to each part. Yes, but do not all imitators try to do this? Not those who produce some large work of sculpture or painting. For if they reproduced the true proportions of beautiful forms, the upper parts, you know, would seem smaller

greek_head: τὰ ἄνω μείζω δὲ τὰ κάτω φαίνοιτ' ἂν διὰ τὸ τὰ μὲν πόρρωθεν τὰ δ' ἐγγύθεν ὑφ' ἡμῶν ὁρᾶσθαι
greek_tokens: o anoos megas de o katos fainw an dia o o men proswqen o de egguqen ufh egw oraw panu men oun ara oun ou xairw o alhqhs eaw e dhmiourgos nun ou o eimi summetria alla o docow eimi kalos o eidwlon enapergazomai pantapasi ge o men ara eteros ou dikaios eoika ge eimi eikwn kalew nai
english_head: and the lower parts larger than they ought, because we see the former from a distance, the latter from near at hand. Certainly. So the artists abandon the truth and give their figures not the actual proportions but those which seem to be beautiful, do they not? Certainly. That, then, which is other, but like, we may fairly call a likeness, may we not? Yes.

greek_head: Καὶ τῆς γε μιμητικῆς τὸ ἐπὶ τούτῳ μέρος κλητέον ὅπερ εἴπομεν ἐν τῷ πρόσθεν εἰκαστικήν
greek_tokens: kai o ge mimhtikos o epi outos meros klhteos osper eipon en o prosqen eikastikos klhteos tis de o fainw men dia o ou ek kalew qea eoika o kalos dunamis de ei tis lambanw o thlikoutos ikanos oraw mhde eoika os fhmi eoika tis kalew ara ou epei fainw men eoika de ou fantasma tis meis oukoun pampolus kai kata o zwgrafia outos o
english_head: And the part of imitation which is concerned with such things, is to be called, as we called it before, likeness-making? It is to be so called. Now then, what shall we call that which appears, because it is seen from an unfavorable position, to be like the beautiful, but which would not even be likely to resemble that which it claims to be like, if a person were able to see such large works adequately? Shall we not call it, since it appears, but is not like, an appearance? Certainly. And this is

greek_head: μέρος ἐστὶ καὶ κατὰ σύμπασαν μιμητικήν Πῶς δ' οὔ
greek_tokens: meros eimi kai kata sumpas mimhtikos pws de ou o dh fantasma alla ou eikwn apergazomai texnh ara ou fantastikos orqos an prosagoreuw polus ge outos toinun o duo elegos eidos o eidwlopoiikos eikastikos kai fantastikos orqos o de ge kai tote amfignoew en poteros o sofisths qeteos oude nun pw dunamai qeaomai safhs
english_head: and in all imitation? Of course. And to the art which produces appearance, but not likeness, the most correct name we could give would be fantastic art, would it not? By all means. These, then, are the two forms of the image-making art that I meant, the likeness-making and the fantastic. You are right. But I was uncertain before in which of the two the sophist should be placed, and even now I cannot see clearly.

greek_head: ἀλλ' ὄντως θαυμαστὸς ἁνὴρ καὶ κατιδεῖν παγχάλεπος ἐπεὶ καὶ νῦν μάλα εὖ καὶ κομψῶς εἰς ἄπορον εἶδος διερευνήσασθαι
greek_tokens: alla ontws qaumastos anhr kai kateidon pagxalepos epei kai nun mala eu kai komyos eis aporos eidos diereunaw katafeugw eoika ara oun autos gignwskw sumfhmi h su oios rumh tis upo o logos suneqizw sunepispaw pros o taxus sumfhmi pws kai pros tis outos erw ontws eimi makarios eimi en pantapasi xaleptw
english_head: The fellow is really wonderful and very difficult to keep in sight, for once more, in the very cleverest manner he has withdrawn into a baffling classification where it is hard to track him. So it seems. Do you assent because you recognize the fact, or did the force of habit hurry you along to a speedy assent? What do you mean, and why did you say that? We are really, my dear friend, engaged in

greek_head: σκέψει τὸ γὰρ φαίνεσθαι τοῦτο καὶ τὸ δοκεῖν εἶναι δὲ μή καὶ τὸ λέγειν μὲν ἄττα ἀληθῆ δὲ μή πάντα ταῦτά ἐστι
greek_tokens: skeptomai o gar fainw outos kai o dokew eimi de mh kai o legw men atta alhqhs de mh pas outos eimi mestos aporia aei en o prosqen xronos kai nun opws gar eipon xrh yeudhs legw h docazw ontws eimi kai outos fqeggomai enantiologia mh sunoxwka pantapasi eimi
english_head: a very difficult investigation; for the matter of appearing and seeming, but not being, and of saying things, but not true ones—all this is now and always has been very perplexing. You see, Theaetetus, it is extremely difficult to understand how a man is to say or think that falsehood really exists and in saying this not be involved

greek_head: Θεαίτητε χαλεπόν Τί δή
greek_tokens: qeaithtos xalepos tis dh tolmaw o logos outos upotiqhmi o mh eimi eimi yeudos gar ou an allos gignomai eimi parmenidhs de o megas eimi pais pais egw eimi arxw te kai dia telos outos apomarturomai pezos te wde ekastote legos kai meta metron ou gar mh pote outos damaw fhmi eimi mh eimi alla su ode apo odaw dizhmai ergw nohma
english_head: in contradiction. Why? This statement involves the bold assumption that not-being exists, for otherwise falsehood could not come into existence. But the great Parmenides, my boy, from the time when we were children to the end of his life, always protested against this and constantly repeated both in prose and in verse:Never let this thought prevail, saith he, that not-being is;But keep your mind from this way of investigation.Parmenides Fr. 7

greek_head: παρ' ἐκείνου τε οὖν μαρτυρεῖται καὶ μάλιστά γε δὴ πάντων ὁ λόγος αὐτὸς ἂν δηλώσειε μέτρια βασανισθείς τοῦτο οὖν
greek_tokens: para ekeinos te oun marturomai kai malista ge dh pas o logos autos an dhlow metrios basanizw outos oun autos proteros qeaomai ei mh tis su diaferw o men emos oph boulomai tiqhmi o de logos h beltistos dieceimi skopaw autos te eimi amos kata outos o odos age alla xrh draw outos kai egw legos o mhdamos eimi tolmaw pou fqeggomai pws gar ou mh toinun eris eneka mhde paidih alla ei spoudazw
english_head: So that is his testimony, and a reasonable examination of the statement itself would make it most absolutely clear. Let us then consider this matter first, if it’s all the same to you. Assume my consent to anything you wish. Consider only the argument, how it may best be pursued; follow your own course, and take me along with you. Very well, then. Now tell me; do we venture to use the phrase absolute not-being? Of course. If, then, not merely for the sake of discussion or as a joke, but

greek_head: δέοι συννοήσαντά τινα ἀποκρίνασθαι τῶν ἀκροατῶν ποῖ χρὴ τοὔνομ' ἐπιφέρειν τοῦτο τὸ μὴ ὄν τί δοκοῦμεν ἂν εἰς τί
greek_tokens: dew sunnoew tis apokrinw o akroaths poi xrh onoma epiferw outos o mh eimi tis dokew an eis tis kai epi poios autos te kataxraw kai o punqanomai deiknumi xalepos arow kai sxedon eipon oios ge egw pantapasi aporos alla oun outos ge dhlos ostis o eimi epi tis o mh eimi ou oisteos pws gar an oukoun epei ou epi o eimi oude epi o tis ferw orqos an tis ferw pws dh
english_head: seriously, one of his pupils were asked to consider and answer the question To what is the designation not-being to be applied? how do we think he would reply to his questioner, and how would he apply the term, for what purpose, and to what object? That is a difficult question; I may say that for a fellow like me it is unanswerable. But this is clear, anyhow, that the term not-being cannot be applied to any being. Of course not. And if not to being, then it could not properly be applied to somet

greek_head: Καὶ τοῦτο ἡμῖν που φανερόν ὡς καὶ τὸ τὶ τοῦτο ῥῆμα ἐπ' ὄντι λέγομεν ἑκάστοτε μόνον γὰρ αὐτὸ λέγειν
greek_tokens: kai outos egw pou faneros ws kai o tis outos rhma epi eimi legw ekastote monos gar autos legw wsper gumnos kai apoerhmow apo o eimi apas adunatos eimi gar adunatos ara ode skopaw sumfhmi ws anagkh o tis legw eis ge tis legw outws eis gar dh o ge tis fhmi shmeion eimi o de tis duo o de tis polus pws gar ou
english_head: And this is plain to us, that we always use the word something of some being, for to speak of something in the abstract, naked, as it were, and disconnected from all beings is impossible, is it not? Yes, it is. You assent because you recognize that he who says something must say some one thing? Yes. And you will agree that something or some in the singular is the sign of one, in the dual of two, and in the plural of many. Of course.

greek_head: Τὸν δὲ δὴ μὴ τὶ λέγοντα ἀναγκαιότατον ὡς ἔοικε παντάπασι μηδὲν λέγειν
greek_tokens: o de dh mh tis legw anagkaios ws eoika pantapasi mhdeis legw anagkaios men oun ara oun oude outos sugxwrhteon o o toioutos legw men tis legw mentoi mhdeis alla oude legw fateon os ge an epixeirew mh eimi fqeggomai telos goun an aporia o logos exw
english_head: And he who says not something, must quite necessarily say absolutely nothing. Quite necessarily. Then we cannot even concede that such a person speaks, but says nothing? We must even declare that he who undertakes to say not-being does not speak at all? The argument could go no further in perplexity.

greek_head: Μήπω μέγ' εἴπῃς ἔτι γάρ ὦ μακάριε ἔστι καὶ ταῦτά γε τῶν ἀποριῶν ἡ μεγίστη καὶ πρώτη περὶ γὰρ
greek_tokens: mhpw megas eipon eti gar eimi makarios eimi kai outos ge o aforizw o megas kai proteros peri gar autos autos o arxh eimi tugxanw pws fhmi legos kai mhdeis apoknew o men eimi pou prosgignomai an tis o eimi eteros pws gar ou mh eimi de tis o eimi ara pote prosgignomai fhmi dunatos eimi kai pws ariqmos dh o sumpas o eimi tiqhmi
english_head: Boast not too soon! For there still remains, my friend, the first and greatest of perplexities. It affects the very beginning of the matter. What do you mean? Do not hesitate to speak. To that which is may be added or attributed some other thing which is? Of course. But shall we assert that to that which is not anything which is can be attributed? Certainly not. Now we assume that all number is among the things which are.

greek_head: Εἴπερ γε καὶ ἄλλο τι θετέον ὡς ὄν Μὴ τοίνυν μηδ' ἐπιχειρῶμεν ἀριθμοῦ μήτε πλῆθος
greek_tokens: eiper ge kai allos tis qeteos ws eimi mh toinun mhde epixeirew ariqmew mhte plhqos mhte eis pros o mh eimi prosferw oukoun an orqos ge ws eoika epixeirew os fhmi o logos pws oun an h dia o stoma fqeggomai an tis h kai o dianoia o parapan lambanw o mh eimi h o mh eimi xwris ariqmew legos ph mh eimi men epeidan legw ara ou plhqos
english_head: Yes, if anything can be assumed to be. Then let us not even undertake to attribute either the singular or the plural of number to not-being. We should, apparently, not be right in undertaking that, as our argument shows. How then could a man either utter in speech or even so much as conceive in his mind things which are not, or not-being, apart from number? Tell me how number is involved in such conceptions. When we say things which are not, do we not attribute

greek_head: ἐπιχειροῦμεν ἀριθμοῦ προστιθέναι Τί μήν
greek_tokens: epixeirew ariqmew prostiqhmi tis meis mh eimi de ara ou o eis au safhs ge kai meis oute dikaios ge oute orqos fhmi eimi epixeirew mh eimi prosarmozw legw alhqhs sunnoew oun ws oute fqeggomai dunatos orqos oute eipon oute dianoeomai o mh eimi autos kaqa eautou alla eimi adianohtos te kai arrhtos kai afqegktos kai alogos pantapasi men oun
english_head: plurality to them? Certainly. And in saying a thing which is not, do we not equally attribute the singular number? Obviously. And yet we assert that it is neither right nor fair to undertake to attribute being to not-being. Very true. Do you see, then, that it is impossible rightly to utter or to say or to think of not-being without any attribute, but it is a thing inconceivable, inexpressible, unspeakable, irrational? Absolutely.

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν ἐψευσάμην ἄρτι λέγων τὴν μεγίστην ἀπορίαν ἐρεῖν αὐτοῦ πέρι τὸ δὲ ἔτι μείζω τινὰ λέγειν ἄλλην ἔχομεν
greek_tokens: ara oun yeudw arti legos o megas aporia erew autos peri o de eti megas tis legw allos exw tis dh eimi qaumasios ou ennoew autos o legw ostis kai o elegxw eis aporia kaqisthmi o mh eimi outws wste opotan autos epixeirew tis elegxw enantios autos eautou peri ekeinos anagkazw legw pws fhmi eipon eti safhs oudeis deomai o safhs en egw skopaw egw men
english_head: Then was I mistaken just now in saying that the difficulty I was going to speak of was the greatest in our subject. But is there a still greater one that we can mention? Why, my dear fellow, don’t you see, by the very arguments we have used, that not-being reduces him who would refute it to such difficulties that when he attempts to refute it he is forced to contradict himself? What do you mean? Speak still more clearly. You must not look for more clearness in me;

greek_head: γὰρ ὑποθέμενος οὔτε ἑνὸς οὔτε τῶν πολλῶν τὸ μὴ ὂν δεῖν μετέχειν ἄρτι τε καὶ νῦν οὕτως ἓν αὐτὸ εἴρηκα τὸ μὴ ὂν
greek_tokens: gar upotiqhmi oute eis oute o polus o mh eimi dew metexw arti te kai nun outws eis autos erw o mh eimi gar fhmi sunihmi su nai kai meis au kai mikros emprosqen afqegktos te autos kai arrhtos kai alogos fhmi eimi sunepomai sunepomai pws gar ou oukoun o ge eimi prosaptw peiraw enantios
english_head: for although I maintained that not-being could have nothing to do with either the singular or the plural number, I spoke of it just now, and am still speaking of it, as one; for I say that which is not. You understand surely? Yes. And again a little while ago I said it was inexpressible, unspeakable, irrational. Do you follow me? Yes, of course. Then when I undertook to attach the verb to be to not-being

greek_head: τοῖς πρόσθεν ἔλεγον Φαίνῃ
greek_tokens: o prosqen elegos fainw tis de outos prosaptw ou ws eis dialegw nai kai meis alogos ge legos kai arrhtos kai afqegktos os ge pros eis o logos poiew pws de ou fhmi de ge dew eiper orqos tis lecis mhte ws eis mhte ws polus diorizw autos mhde o parapan autos kalew eis gar eidomai kai kata outos an o prosrhsis prosagoreuw pantapasi ge
english_head: I was contradicting what I said before. Evidently. Well, then; when I attached this verb to it, did I not address it in the singular? Yes. And when I called it irrational, inexpressible, and unspeakable, I addressed my speech to it as singular. Of course you did. But we say that, if one is to speak correctly, one must not define it as either singular or plural, and must not even call it it at all; for even by this manner of referring to it one would be giving it the form of the singular. Certain

greek_head: Τὸν μὲν τοίνυν ἐμέ γε τί τις ἂν λέγοι καὶ γὰρ πάλαι καὶ τὰ νῦν ἡττημένον ἂν εὕροι περὶ τὸν τοῦ μὴ ὄντος
greek_tokens: o men toinun egw ge tis tis an legos kai gar pala kai o nun hssaomai an euriskw peri o o mh eimi elegxos wste en egw legw kaqa eipon mh skopaw o orqologia peri o mh eimi alla eia dh nun en sos skeptomai pws fhmi eimi egw eu kai gennaios ate neos eimi ostis malista dunamai sunteinw peiraw mhte ousia mhte o eis mhte plhqos ariqmew prostiqhmi o mh eimi kata o orqos fqeggomai tis peri autos
english_head: But poor me, what can anyone say of me any longer? For you would find me now, as always before, defeated in the refutation of not-being. So, as I said before, we must not look to me for correctness of speech about not-being. But come now, let us look to you for it. What do you mean? Come, I beg of you, make a sturdy effort, young man as you are, and try with might and main to say something correctly about not-being, without attributing to it either existence or unity or plurality.

greek_head: Πολλὴ μεντἄν με καὶ ἄτοπος ἔχοι προθυμία τῆς ἐπιχειρήσεως εἰ σὲ τοιαῦθ' ὁρῶν πάσχοντα αὐτὸς
greek_tokens: polus mentoi egw kai atopos exw proqumia o epixeirhsis ei sos toioutos oraw pasxw autos epixeirew alla ei dokew sos men kai egw xairw eaw ews de an tis dunamai draw outos entugxanw mexri outos legw ws pas mallon panourgos eis aporos o sofisths topos kataduw kai mala dh fainw toigar ei tis fhmi autos exw fantastikos
english_head: But I should be possessed of great and absurd eagerness for the attempt, if I were to undertake it with your experience before my eyes. Well, if you like, let us say no more of you and me; but until we find someone who can accomplish this, let us confess that the sophist has in most rascally fashion hidden himself in a place we cannot explore. That seems to be decidedly the case. And so, if we say he has an art, as it were, of making appearances,

greek_head: τέχνην ῥᾳδίως ἐκ ταύτης τῆς χρείας τῶν λόγων ἀντιλαμβανόμενος ἡμῶν εἰς τοὐναντίον ἀποστρέψει τοὺς λόγους ὅταν
greek_tokens: texnh radios ek outos o xreia o logos antilambanw egw eis enantion apostrefw o logos otan eidwlopoios autos kalew anerwtaw tis pote o parapan eidwlon legw skopaw oun eimi qeaithtos xrh tis tis o neanihs pros o erwtaw apokrinw dhlos ostis fhmi o te en o udwr kai katoptron eidwlon eti kai o grafw kai o tupow kai allos osos pou toioutos esqw eteros
english_head: he will easily take advantage of our poverty of terms to make a counter attack, twisting our words to the opposite meaning; when we call him an image-maker, he will ask us what we mean by image, exactly. So, Theaetetus, we must see what reply is to be made to the young man’s question. Obviously we shall reply that we mean the images in water and in mirrors, and those in paintings, too, and sculptures, and all the other things of the same sort.

greek_head: Φανερός ὦ Θεαίτητε εἶ σοφιστὴν οὐχ ἑωρακώς Τί δή
greek_tokens: faneros eimi qeaithtos ei sofisths ou oraw tis dh docis su muw h pantapasi ou exw omma pws o apokrisls otan outws autos didwmi ean en katoptron h plasma legos tis katagelaw su o logos otan ws blepw legos autos prospoiew oute katoptron
english_head: It is evident, Theaetetus, that you never saw a sophist. Why? He will make you think his eyes are shut or he has none at all. How so? When you give this answer, if you speak of something in mirrors or works of art, he will laugh at your words, when you talk to him as if he could see.

greek_head: οὔτε ὕδατα γιγνώσκειν οὔτε τὸ παράπαν ὄψιν τὸ δ' ἐκ τῶν λόγων ἐρωτήσει σε μόνον
greek_tokens: oute udwr gignwskw oute o parapan oyis o de ek o logos erwthsis su monos poios o dia pas outos o polus eipon aciow eis proseipon onoma fqeggomai eidwlon epi pas ws eis eimi legos oun kai amunw mhdeis upoxwrew o anhr tis dhta eimi cenos eidwlon an fhmi eimi plhn ge o pros alhqinos afomoiow eteros toioutos eteros de legw toioutos alhqinos h epi tis o
english_head: He will feign ignorance of mirrors and water and of sight altogether, and will question you only about that which is deduced from your words. What is that? That which exists throughout all these things which you say are many but which you saw fit to call by one name, when you said image of them all, as if they were all one thing. So speak and defend yourself. Do not give way to the man at all. Why, Stranger, what can we say an image is, except another such thing fashioned in the likeness of the

greek_head: τοιοῦτον εἶπες Οὐδαμῶς ἀληθινόν γε ἀλλ' ἐοικὸς μέν
greek_tokens: toioutos eipon oudamos alhqinos ge alla eoika men ara o alhqinos ontws eimi legos outws tis de o mh alhqinos ara enantion alhqhs tis meis ou ontws ou eimi ara legw o eoika eiper autos ge mh alhqinos erew alla eimi ge meis pws oukoun alhqhs ge fhmi ou gar oun plhn ge eikwn ontws ou eimi ara ou ontws eimi ontws os legw eikwn
english_head: in what sense did you say such? Not a true one by any means, but only one like the true. And by the true you mean that which really is? Exactly. And the not true is the opposite of the true? Of course. That which is like, then, you say does not really exist, if you say it is not true. But it does exist, in a way. But not truly, you mean. No, except that it is really a likeness. Then what we call a likeness, though not really existing, really does exist?

greek_head: Κινδυνεύει τοιαύτην τινὰ πεπλέχθαι συμπλοκὴν τὸ μὴ ὂν τῷ ὄντι καὶ μάλα ἄτοπον
greek_tokens: kinduneuw toioutos tis plekw sumplokh o mh eimi o eimi kai mala atopos pws gar ou atopos oraw goun ostis kai nun dia o epallacis outos o polukefalos sofisths anagkazw egw o mh eimi ou ekwn omologew eimi pws oraw kai mala tis de dh o texnh autos tis aforizw egw autos sumfwnew oios te eimi ph kai o poios tis fobew outws legw
english_head: Not-being does seem to have got into some such entanglement with being, and it is very absurd. Of course it is absurd. You see, at any rate, how by this interchange of words the many-headed sophist has once more forced us against our will to admit that not-being exists in a way. Yes, I see that very well. Well then, how can we define his art without contradicting ourselves? Why do you say that? What are you afraid of?

greek_head: Ὅταν περὶ τὸ φάντασμα αὐτὸν ἀπατᾶν φῶμεν καὶ τὴν τέχνην εἶναί τινα ἀπατητικὴν αὐτοῦ τότε πότερον ψευδῆ
greek_tokens: otan peri o fantasma autos apath fhmi kai o texnh eimi tis apathtikos autos tote poteros yeudhs docazw o yuxh egw fhmi upo o ekeinos texnh h tis pote erew outos tis gar an allos eipon yeudhs de au doca eimi enantios o eimi docazw h pws outws enantios legw ara o mh eimi docazw o yeudhs doca anagkh
english_head: When, in talking about appearance, we say that he deceives and that his art is an art of deception, shall we say that our mind is misled by his art to hold a false opinion, or what shall we say? We shall say that. What else could we say? But, again, false opinion will be that which thinks the opposite of reality, will it not? Yes. You mean, then, that false opinion thinks things which are not? Necessarily.

greek_head: Πότερον μὴ εἶναι τὰ μὴ ὄντα δοξάζουσαν ἤ πως εἶναι τὰ μηδαμῶς ὄντα
greek_tokens: poteros mh eimi o mh eimi docazw h pws eimi o mhdamos eimi eimi pws o mh eimi deomai ge eiper yeudw pote tis tis kai kata braxus tis de ou kai mhdamos eimi o pantws eimi docazw nai kai outos dh yeudos kai outos kai logos oimh yeudhs outws kata tautos nomizw
english_head: Does it think that things which are not, are not, or that things which are not at all, in some sense are? It must think that things which are not in some sense are—that is, if anyone is ever to think falsely at all, even in a slight degree. And does it not also think that things which certainly are, are not at all? Yes. And this too is falsehood? Yes, it is. And therefore a statement will likewise be considered false,

greek_head: τά τε ὄντα λέγων μὴ εἶναι καὶ τὰ μὴ ὄντα εἶναι Πῶς γὰρ ἂν ἄλλως τοιοῦτος γένοιτο
greek_tokens: o te eimi legos mh eimi kai o mh eimi eimi pws gar an allos toioutos gignomai sxedon oudamos alla outos o sofisths ou fhmi h tis mhxanh sugxwrew tis o eu fronew otan afqegktos kai arrhtos kai alogos kai adianohtos prosdiomologew eimi o pro outos omologew manqanw eimi qeaithtos o legw pws gar ou manqanw ostis enantios fhmi legw egw o nundi yeudhs tolmaw eipon ws eimi
english_head: if it declares that things which are, are not, or that things which are not, are. In what other way could a statement be made false? Virtually in no other way; but the sophist will not assent to this. Or how can any reasonable man assent to it, when the expressions we just agreed upon were previously agreed to be inexpressible, unspeakable, irrational, and inconceivable? Do we understand his meaning, Theaetetus? Of course we understand that he will say we are contradicting our recent statements,

greek_head: ἐν δόξαις τε καὶ κατὰ λόγους τῷ γὰρ μὴ ὄντι τὸ ὂν προςάπτειν ἡμᾶς πολλάκις ἀναγκάζεσθαι διομολογησαμένους
greek_tokens: en doca te kai kata logos o gar mh eimi o eimi prosaptw egw pollakis anagkazw diomologew nundi outos eimi pas adunatos orqos apomnhmoneuw alla oraw dh bouleuw tis xrh draw o sofisths peri o gar antilhyis kai aporia ean autos diereunaw en o o yeudourgos kai gohs texnh tiqhmi oraw ws euporos kai polus kai mala mikros meros toinun autos dierxomai eimi
english_head: for he will say that we are thus forced repeatedly to attribute being to not-being, although we agreed a while ago that nothing could be more impossible than that. You are quite right to remind me. But I think it is high time to consider what ought to be done about the sophist; for you see how easily and repeatedly he can raise objections and difficulties, if we conduct our search by putting him in the guild of false-workers and jugglers. Very true. Yes, we have gone through only a small part of

greek_head: ὡς ἔπος εἰπεῖν ἀπεράντων Ἀδύνατόν γ' ἄν ὡς ἔοικεν εἴη τὸν σοφιστὴν
greek_tokens: ws epos eipon aperantos adunatos ge an ws eoika eaw o sofisths airew ei outos outws exis tis oun outws afisthmi nun malqakizomai oukoun egw fhmi dew ei kai kata mikros oios su epilambanw ph anhr eimi ecis oun suggnwmh kai kaqa nun eipon agaphsis ean ph kai kata braxus paraspaw outws isxuros logos pws gar ou exw
english_head: and they are, if I may say so, infinite. It would, apparently, be impossible to catch the sophist, if that is the case. Well, then, shall we weaken and give up the struggle now? No, I say; we must not do that, if we can in any way get the slightest hold of the fellow. Will you then pardon me, and, as your words imply, be content if I somehow withdraw just for a short distance from this strong argument of his? Of course I will.

greek_head: Τόδε τοίνυν ἔτι μᾶλλον παραιτοῦμαί σε Τὸ ποῖον
greek_tokens: ode toinun eti mallon paraiteomai su o poios mh egw oios patraloias upolambanw gignomai tis tis dh o o pathr parmenidhs logos anagkaion egw amunw eimi basanizw kai biazw o te mh eimi ws eimi kata tis kai o eimi au palin ws ou eimi ph fainw o toioutos diamaxeteon en o logos pws gar ou fainw kai o legw dh outos tuflos
english_head: I have another still more urgent request to make of you. What is it? Do not assume that I am becoming a sort of parricide. What do you mean? In defending myself I shall have to test the theory of my father Parmenides, and contend forcibly that after a fashion not-being is and on the other hand in a sense being is not. It is plain that some such contention is necessary. Yes, plain even to a blind man, as they say; for unless these statements

greek_head: τούτων γὰρ μήτ' ἐλεγχθέντων μήτε ὁμολογηθέντων σχολῇ ποτέ τις οἷός τε ἔσται περὶ λόγων ψευδῶν λέγων ἢ δόξης
greek_tokens: outos gar mhte elegxw mhte omologew sxolazw pote tis oios te eimi peri logos yeudos legos h doca eite eidwlon eite eikwn eite mimhma eite fantasma autos h kai peri texnh o osos peri outos eimi mh katagelastos eimi o ge enantios anagkazw eautou legw alhqhs
english_head: are either disproved or accepted, no one who speaks about false words, or false opinion—whether images or likenesses or imitations or appearances—about the arts which have to do with them, can ever help being forced to contradict himself and make himself ridiculous. Very true.

greek_head: Διὰ ταῦτα μέντοι τολμητέον ἐπιτίθεσθαι τῷ πατρικῷ λόγῳ νῦν ἢ τὸ παράπαν ἐατέον εἰ τοῦτό τις εἴργει
greek_tokens: dia outos mentoi tolmhteon epitiqhmi o patrikos logos nun h o parapan eateos ei outos tis ergw draw oknos alla egw outos ge mhdeis mhdamh ergw tritos toinun eti su mikros tis paraiteomai legos monos eipon pou nundi legos ws pros o peri outos elegxos aei te apoerw egw tugxanw kai dh kai o nun eipon fobew dh o erw mh pote dia outos su manikos eimi docow para pous metaballw emautou anoos kai
english_head: And so we must take courage and attack our father’s theory here and now, or else, if any scruples prevent us from doing this, we must give the whole thing up. But nothing in the world must prevent us. Then I have a third little request to make of you. You have only to utter it. I said a while ago that I always have been too faint-hearted for the refutation of this theory, and so I am now. Yes, so you did. english_head: If we speak in this way and make this request of them and of all who say that the universe is more than one, shall we, my boy, be doing anything improper? Not in the least. Well then, must we not, so far as we can, try to learn from those who say that the universe is one what they mean when they say being? Of course we must. Then let them answer this question: Do you say that one only is? We do, they will say; will they not? Yes. Well then, do you give the name of being to anything? Yes.

Our first duty would be to make them really better, if it were in any way possible; but if this cannot be done, let us pretend that they are better, by assuming that they would be willing to answer more in accordance with the rules of dialectic than they actually are. For the acknowledgement of anything by better men is more valid than if made by worse

english_head: Quite right. Now tell them, assuming that they have become better, to answer you, and do you interpret what they say. I will do so. Let them tell whether they say there is such a thing as a mortal animal. Of course they do. And they agree that this is a body with a soul in it, do they not? Certainly. Giving to soul a place among things which exist?

greek_head: Ναί Τί δέ ψυχὴν οὐ τὴν μὲν δικαίαν τὴν δὲ ἄδικόν

english_head: Yes. Well then, do they not say that one soul is just and another unjust, one wise and another foolish? Of course. And do they not say that each soul becomes just by the possession and presence of justice, and the opposite by the possession and presence of the opposite? Yes, they agree to this also. But surely they will say that that which is capable of becoming present or absent exists. Yes, they say that.

english_head: Granting, then, that justice and wisdom and virtue in general and their opposites exist, and also, of course, the soul in which they become present, do they say that any of these is visible and tangible, or that they are all invisible? For when he says that, he makes a statement about that which is or is becoming or has become or is to be; he does not merely give names, but he reaches a conclusion by combining verbs with nouns. That is why we said that he discourses and does not merely give names, and therefore we gave to this combination the name of discourse. That was right. So, then, just as of things some fit each other and some do not, so too some vocal signs do not fit,

A sentence, if it is to be a sentence, must have a subject; without a subject it is impossible. True. And it must also be of some quality, must it not? Of course. Now let us pay attention to each other. Yes, at any rate we ought to do so. english_head: I will, to the best of my ability. Theaetetus sits. It isn’t a long sentence, is it? No, it is fairly short. Now it is for you to say what it is about and what its subject is. Clearly it is about me, and I am its subject. And how about this sentence? What one? Theaetetus, with whom I am now talking, flies. Every one would agree that this also is about me and I am its subject. But we agree that every sentence must have some quality.

You will understand more easily if you first grap their natures

english_head: and the several differences between them. Give me an opportunity. Well, then, thought and speech are the same; only the former, which is a silent inner conversation of the soul with itself, has been given the special name of thought. Is not that true? Certainly. But the stream that flows from the soul in vocal utterance through the mouth has the name of speech? True. And in speech we know there is just— What? Affirmation and negation Yes, we know that.

greek_head: Ὅταν οὖν τοῦτο ἐν ψυχῇ κατὰ διάνοιαν ἐγγίγνηται μετὰ σιγῆς πλὴν δόξης ἔχεις ὅτι προσείπῃς αὐτό

english_head: Now when this arises in the soul silently by way of thought, can you give it any other name than opinion? Certainly not. And when such a condition is brought about in anyone, not independently, but through sensation, can it properly be called anything but seeming, or fancy? No. Then since speech, as we found, is true and false, and we saw that thought is conversation of the soul with itself, and opinion is the final result of thought,

greek_head: δόξα δὲ διανοίας ἀποτελεύτησις φαίνεται δὲ ὃ λέγομεν σύμμειξις αἰσθήσεως καὶ δόξης ἀνάγκη δὴ καὶ τούτων τῷ

english_head: and what we mean when we say it seems is a mixture of sensation and opinion, it is inevitable that, since these are all akin to speech, some of them must sometimes be false. Certainly. Do you see, then, that false opinion and false discourse were found sooner than we expected when we feared a few moments ago that in looking for them we were undertaking an endless task? Yes, I see. Then let us not be discouraged about the rest of our search, either;

english_head: for now that these points are settled, we have only to revert to our previous divisions into classes. What divisions? We made two classes of image-making, the likeness-making and the fantastic. Yes. And we said that we did not know to which of the two the sophist should be assigned. You are right. But now, since the existence of false speech and false opinion has been proved, it is possible for imitations of realities to exist and for an art of deception to arise from this condition of mind. Yes, it is possible. And we decided some time ago that the sophist was in one of those two divisions of the image-making class. Yes. Then let us try again; let us divide in two

english_head: to ourselves and secondly to those who are most closely akin to the dialectic method. Right. We began by making two divisions of art, the productive and the acquisitive, did we not? Yes. And the sophist showed himself to us in the arts of hunting, contests, commerce, and the like, which were subdivisions of acquisitive art? Certainly. But now, since imitative art has taken him over, it is clear that our first step must be the division of productive art into two parts;

greek_head: ἡ γάρ που μίμησις ποίησίς τίς ἐστιν εἰδώλων μέντοι φαμέν ἀλλ' οὐκ αὐτῶν ἑκάστων ἦ γάρ

english_head: for imitative art is a kind of production—of images, however, we say, not of real things in each case. Do you agree? By all means. Then let us first assume two parts of productive art. What are they? The divine and the human. I don’t yet understand. We said, if we remember the beginning of our conversation, that every power is productive which causes things to come into being which did not exist before. Yes, we remember.

Well said, Theaetetus; and if I thought you were one of those who would think differently by and by, I should try now, by argument and urgent persuasion, to make you agree with my opinion; but since I understand your nature and see that it of itself inclines,

english_head: without any words of mine, towards that to which you say you are at present attracted, I will let that go; for it would be a waste of time. But I will assume that things which people call natural are made by divine art, and things put together by man out of those as materials are made by human art, and that there are accordingly two kinds of art, the one human and the other divine. Quite right. Now that there are two, divide each of them again. How?

greek_head: Οἷον τότε μὲν κατὰ πλάτος τέμνων τὴν ποιητικὴν πᾶσαν νῦν δὲ αὖ κατὰ μῆκος

english_head: You divided all productive art widthwise, as it were, before; now divide it lengthwise. Assume that it is done. In that way we now get four parts in all; two belong to us and are human, and two belong to the gods and are divine. Yes. And again, when the section is made the other way, one part of each half has to do with the making of real things, and the two remaining parts may very well be called image-making; and so productive art is again divided into two parts.

And in the same way, we say, all the other works of our creative activity also are twofold and go in pairs—the thing itself, produced by the art that creates real things, and the image, produced by the image-making art. english_head: the one who does not know from the one who knows. Yes. Where, then, can the fitting name for each of the two be found? Clearly it is not an easy task, because there was, it seems, among the earlier thinkers a long established and careless indolence in respect to the division of classes or genera into forms or species, so that nobody even tried to make such divisions; therefore there cannot be a great abundance of names. english_head: and that which is founded on knowledge, a sort of scientific imitation. Agreed. We mat therefore apply ourselves to the former, for we found that the sophist was among those who imitate but was not among those who know. Very true. Then let us examine the opinion-imitator as if he were a piece of iron, and see whether he is sound or there is still some seam in him. Let us do so.

english_head: Well, there is a very marked seam. For some of these imitators are simple-minded and think they know that about which they have only opinion, but the other kind because of their experience in the rough and tumble of arguments, strongly suspect and fear that they are ignorant of the things which they pretend before the public to know. Certainly the two classes you mention both exist. Then shall we call one the simple imitator and the other the dissembling imitator? That is reaonable, at any rate.

You are quite right. And what name shall we give to him who makes the longer speeches? Statesman or popular orator? Popular orator. And what shall we call the other? Philosopher or sophist?
